query:
How much time has passed between Blake's night with Eldoria and his search for Sabrina York in his mind-world?

 (A) 7 years
 (B) 10 hours
 (C) 12 years
 (D) 1 hour
references: 10 hours

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
Presently the girl returned, and not long afterward the cloying scent of native incense crept beneath the arras and permeated the anteroom. She sat sideways on the mat this time, and he caught her face in profile. There was a suggestion of saintliness in the line of the nose and chin, a suggestion made all the more poignant by the slender column of the neck. He shifted uncomfortably on the guest mat. She had taken up the
Anabasis
again, and silence was pounding silent fists upon the walls.
He was relieved when Eldoria finally arrived. She ushered him into the next room immediately. It was slightly larger than the anteroom, and much more richly appointed. A thick carpet the color of Martian waterways lay upon the floor, contrasting pleasantly with the golden tapestries that adorned all four walls. The sleeping dais was oval and took up nearly half the floor space. It was strewn with scarlet cushions.
Blake sat down upon it. Nervously he watched Eldoria slip out of her white street robe, his eyes moving back and forth from her smooth dark skin to the arras. The incense thickened around him.
She noticed the back-and-forth movement of his eyes. "You need not fear the little one," she said, laying her hand upon his knee. "She will not enter."
"It's not that so much," Blake said.
"What?" The warm bronze shoulder was touching his....
He rose up once in the night, thinking to find his hotel bed. His next awakening was in the grayness of dawn, and he got up and dressed and moved silently to the doorway. The girl slept just without the arras on a thin sleeping-mat, and he had to step over her to gain the anteroom. In sleep, a strand of her copper-colored hair had tumbled down across her forehead and lay like a lovely flower upon the virginal whiteness of her skin. There was something saintly about her quiet face.

read_agent_gists:
THE GIRL IN HIS MIND
By ROBERT F. YOUNG

Nathan Blake watched a chocoletto girl perform a dance hinting at the kylee sex ritual of Dubhe 7. Despite its expurgation, it was still provocative. Her G-string barely covered her, and Blake found her movements captivating. He asked the waiter if she was free and, after the dance, beckoned her to his table. She joined him, ordered wine, and asked for payment for a night. Blake agreed, and she gave him her hut number.

Blake, after buying whiskey, went to her hut. Instead of a hanger-on, he found a human girl reading Xenophon's Anabasis. She asked if he was there for Eldoria, and Blake nodded.

She laughed, "I live here. My parents died on the Dubhe 4 plantations, and I was auctioned off. Eldoria bought me." Blake, hardened by years as a psycheye, found the situation sickening. "How old are you?" "Fourteen. Eldoria is sending me to mission school and will give me freedom when I come of age." Blake asked about her studies. "I study humanities—Xenophon, Plato, Homer, Virgil." "Deirdre," she said. "Nathan Blake." She left to prepare Eldoria's dais. Shame flared in Blake's cheeks, but he stayed.

The girl returned, and the scent of incense filled the room. She sat sideways, and he noticed a saintly quality in her profile. He shifted uncomfortably as she read a book, relieved when Eldoria arrived and led him to a larger, more luxurious room. Blake sat nervously as Eldoria removed her robe, and she reassured him about the girl. In the night, he awoke and stepped over the sleeping girl, noticing her saintly appearance.

When he reached the alley, he ran until the chocoletto sector was far behind. The hill, steep like Aldebaran 12's, left him breathless at the crest. Ahead was a memory of Deneb 1 wasteland, a terrain he wished he hadn't remembered. His pursuers, barely visible in the rain, seemed closer. They had been trailing him for ten hours, but he couldn't face them. After resting, he crossed the detailed wasteland, following clear footprints. Sabrina York, unaware of mind-tracker tricks, thought hiding in her tracker's mind was enough, unaware Blake had found her.

Mind-country was chaotic, blending times and places. Blake saw a boyhood meadow next to a wasteland, followed by his later house, flanked by a spaceport and city-block, with a Martian waterway behind. Sabrina's footsteps led to the ajar front door. Inside, his father smoked, unaware, while 3V failed to play. In the kitchen, Blake's mother prepared an old meal, and he saw a name-plate reading "Sabrina York".

He stepped back, uneasy after seeing Sabrina York's name on the kitchen range. Systematically searching the house, he found no sign of her. At the stairs, he spotted his pursuers in the meadow and hesitated, surprised they were women. Controlling his impulse to flee, he left by the back door, following Sabrina's trail to a bench under an elm tree. Pausing by the trunk, memories of Deirdre Eldoria's graduation and the diamond brooch she wore stirred painful emotions.

Deirdre insisted on Blake coming to her prom at nine. Blake, thirty-eight, refused, saying proms were for parents or the young man she talked to earlier. Deirdre argued she wasn't his daughter and he hadn't supported her education. Blake admitted buying her after her real father died was just to ease his conscience. Deirdre, frustrated, said she was leaving for New Earth tomorrow and they'd be apart for years. Blake offered to visit, but Deirdre knew he wouldn't come, ending the argument.

Blake Past stood up. "I'll walk back to the sorority house with you."
She tossed her head, but her sadness showed in her eyes. "If you wish," she said.
Blake Present watched them walk away, feeling pain. He turned away, seeing three shadows at his feet.
Amazed, shocked, and then fearful, he faced Miss Stoddart, Officer Finch, and Vera Velvetskin. They hated him, their faces thin and eyes dark. He realized they weren't just memories but something else in his mind.
"Callous creature!" "Sought sanctuary!" they accused in unison. "You know who we are!"
Blake stared, then fled.

Man discovered he could create universes within his mind, a feat first achieved by psychologist Trevor when he entered a patient's unconscious memory. This led to the founding of a new psychological school and a method for anyone to enter their own mind-world through the practice of projecting into past moments. Mind-countries were confusing, existing on a subjective reality plane connected only through the awareness of their creators.

It was inevitable that criminals would hide in their own mind-worlds until the statute of limitations ran out, leading to the creation of psyche-police and private psycheyes like Blake. In a unique case, a criminal named Sabrina York hid in Blake's mind, using a memory of his old office. However, her presence was revealed when Blake noticed the disarray in the mental image of the office and found her handkerchief. Now at her mercy, Blake pursued her, though he was puzzled about how a stranger knew enough about his past to use it against him.

The urgent issue was that he knew people could create advanced mind-beings beyond ordinary ghosts. He had seen a woman create a living Virgin Mary and an ex-soldier with a ten-star general in his mind. Assuming Blake was less well-adjusted, why did he create three malevolent super-images: Miss Stoddart, Officer Finch, and Vera Velvetskin? They followed him to a memory of Walden Pond and an English park memorializing poets, where Burns' birthplace stood out vividly.

Sabrina York's footprints showed she entered and left the place. Blake, despite feeling repugnance, lingered and entered. Inside, he saw a lived-in room with a man in a chair, a bottle of wine on the table, and signs of long-term habitation like dirt and smoke damage.

query:
Why does Deirdre get so upset when Blake Past suggests she go to prom with the young man?

 (A) Because Blake is trying to guilt Deirdre into going with the young man by telling her that it'll ease her conscience. 
 (B) Because Deirdre has fallen in love with Blake, despite his age, and wants him to take her to the prom.  
 (C) Because Blake is acting like he's her father, which is a sensitive topic for Deirdre because she lost her real parents. 
 (D) Because the young man gave up his right arm in order to afford tickets to the prom, and this disgusts Deirdre.
references: Because Deirdre has fallen in love with Blake, despite his age, and wants him to take her to the prom.

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
Deirdre was speaking. "Yes," she was saying, "at nine o'clock. And I should very much like for you to come."
Blake Past shook his head. "Proms aren't for parents. You know that as well as I do. That young man you were talking with a few minutes ago—he's the one who should take you. He'd give his right arm for the chance."
"I'll thank you not to imply that you're my father. One would think from the way you talk that you are centuries old!"
"I'm thirty-eight," Blake Past said, "and while I may not be your father, I'm certainly old enough to be. That young man—"
A pink flush of anger climbed into Deirdre Eldoria's girlish cheeks. "What right has
he
got to take me! Did
he
scrimp and go without in order to put me through high school and college? Has
he
booked passage for me to New Earth and paid my tuition to Trevor University?"
"Please," Blake Past said, desperation deepening his voice. "You're only making everything worse. After majoring in Trevorism, you certainly ought to realize by now that there was nothing noble about my buying you after Eldoria died. I only did it to ease my conscience—"
"What do
you
know about conscience?" Deirdre demanded. "Conscience is a much more complex mechanism than most laymen realize. Guilt feelings aren't reliable criteria. They can stem from false causes—from ridiculous things like a person's inability to accept himself for what he is." Abruptly she dropped the subject. "Don't you realize, Nate," she went on a little desperately, "that I'm leaving tomorrow and that we won't see each other again for years and years?"
"I'll come to New Earth to visit you," Blake said. "Venus is only a few days distant on the new ships."
She stood up. "You won't come—I know you won't." She stamped her foot. "And you won't come to the prom either. I know that too. I knew it all along. Sometimes I'm tempted to—" Abruptly she broke off. "Very well then," she went on, "I'll say good-by now then."

She laughed. "I am here because I live here," she said.
He tried to assimilate the information, but could not. Perceiving his difficulty, the girl went on, "My parents indentured themselves to the Great Starway Cartel and were assigned to the rubber plantations of Dubhe 4. They died of yellow-water dysentery before their indenture ran out, and in accordance with Interstellar Law I was auctioned off along with the rest of their possessions. Eldoria bought me."
Five years as a roving psycheye had hardened Blake to commercial colonization practices; nevertheless, he found the present example of man's inhumanity to man sickening.
"How old are you?" Blake asked.
"Fourteen."
"And what are you going to be when you grow up?"
"Probably I shall be a psychiatrist. Eldoria is sending me to the mission school now, and afterward she is going to put me through an institute of higher learning. And when I come of age, she is going to give me my freedom."
"I see," Blake said. He indicated the book on her lap. "Homework?"
She shook her head. "In addition to my courses at the mission school, I am studying the humanities."
"Xenophon," Blake said. "And I suppose Plato too."
"And Homer and Virgil and Aeschylus and Euripides and all the rest of them. When I grow up I shall be a most well-educated person."
"I'm sure you will be," Blake said, looking at the arras.
"My name is Deirdre."
"Nathan," Blake said. "Nathan Blake."
"Eldoria will be arriving soon. I must go and prepare her dais."
She got up, parted the arras, and slipped into the next room. Shame flamed in Blake's cheeks, and for a moment he considered leaving; then he remembered Eldoria's dance, and he went right on sitting where he was.

read_agent_gists:
THE GIRL IN HIS MIND
By ROBERT F. YOUNG

Nathan Blake watched a chocoletto girl perform a dance hinting at the kylee sex ritual of Dubhe 7. Despite its expurgation, it was still provocative. Her G-string barely covered her, and Blake found her movements captivating. He asked if she was free and, after her dance, invited her to his table. She agreed for 3,000 quandoes, and he went to wait at her hut.

Upon arrival, Blake found a human girl reading Xenophon's Anabasis instead of the expected chocoletto hanger-on. She asked if he was there for Eldoria, and Blake nodded.

She laughed, "I live here. My parents died on the Dubhe 4 plantations, and I was auctioned off. Eldoria bought me." Blake, hardened by years as a psycheye, found the situation sickening. "How old are you?" "Fourteen. Eldoria is sending me to school and will free me when I come of age." Blake noted her studying humanities. "Xenophon?" "And more." She introduced herself as Deirdre, then left to prepare Eldoria's dais, leaving Blake feeling ashamed.

The girl returned, and the scent of incense filled the room. She sat sideways, and he noticed a saintly quality in her profile. He shifted uncomfortably as she read a book, relieved when Eldoria arrived and moved him to a larger, more luxurious room. Blake sat nervously as Eldoria undressed, noting the incense thickened around him. She reassured him about the girl, but he rose in the night, stepping over her sleeping form in the dawn.

When he reached the alley, he ran until the chocoletto sector was far behind. The hill, steep like Aldebaran 12's, left him breathless at the crest. Ahead was a memory of Deneb 1 wasteland, a terrain he wished he hadn't remembered. His pursuers, barely visible in the rain, seemed closer. They had been trailing him for ten hours, but he couldn't face them. After resting, he crossed the detailed wasteland, following clear footprints. Sabrina York, unaware of mind-tracker tricks, thought hiding in her tracker's mind was enough, unaware Blake had found her.

Mind-country was chaotic, blending times and places. Blake saw a boyhood meadow next to a wasteland, followed by a house from a later time, surrounded by diverse galactic landscapes. The house was flanked by a spaceport and a city-block, with a Martian waterway behind it. Sabrina's footsteps led to the front door, ajar. Blake entered, finding his father smoking and watching 3V, unaware of Blake's presence. In the kitchen, Blake saw his mother preparing a meal from twenty-three years ago. He noticed a name-plate on the range reading "Sabrina York."

He stepped back, uneasy after seeing Sabrina York's name on the kitchen range. Systematically searching the house, he found no sign of her. At the stairs, he spotted his pursuers in the meadow and hesitated, surprised they were women. Controlling his fear, he left through the back door, following Sabrina's trail to a bench under an elm tree. Pausing by the trunk, memories of Deirdre's graduation and their past together overwhelmed him.

Deirdre insisted on Blake coming to her prom at nine. Blake, thirty-eight, refused, saying proms were for parents or the young man she talked to earlier. Deirdre argued she wasn't his daughter and he hadn't supported her education. Blake admitted buying her after her real father died was just to ease his conscience. Deirdre, about to leave for New Earth, pleaded with Blake, knowing he wouldn't come.

Blake Past stood up. "I'll walk back to the sorority house with you."
She tossed her head, but her sadness showed in her eyes. "If you wish," she said.
Blake Present watched them walk away, feeling pain. He turned away, seeing three shadows at his feet.
Amazed, shocked, and then fearful, he faced Miss Stoddart, Officer Finch, and Vera Velvetskin. They hated him, their faces thin and eyes dark. He realized they weren't just memories but something else in his mind.
"Callous creature!" "Sought sanctuary!" they accused in unison. "You know who we are, Nathan Blake!"
Blake stared, then fled.

Man discovered he could create universes within his mind, a feat first achieved by psychologist Trevor when he entered a patient's unconscious memory. This led to the founding of a new psychological school and the development of a method to project oneself into past moments, known as "place-time." While entering another's mind-world required intensive study, the technique could be mastered with practice. These mind-countries were confusing, bearing no apparent relation to the objective universe, and their shapes and images varied based on individual interpretation.

It was inevitable that criminals would hide in their own mind-worlds until the statute of limitations ran out, leading to the creation of psyche-police and private psycheyes like Blake. In a first-ever case, a criminal, Sabrina York, hid in Blake's mind. Her entry into a sentimental memory of Blake's old office gave her away. Retiring to his quarters, Blake pursued her there. Her only advantage lost, she was at his mercy unless she found him first. Blake was troubled by how Sabrina knew about his old office and worried she might have created a superior mental being to aid her.

Blake created three malevolent super-images: Miss Stoddart, Officer Finch, and Vera Velvetskin. They followed him to a memory-image of Walden Pond, where they expressed delight, especially at Thoreau's shack. The scene shifted to an English park memorializing poets, with Burns' birthplace standing out vividly. Blake lingered despite feeling repugnance, remembering the cottage's details clearly.
He paused, seeing a man in the chair and a wine bottle on the table. The room showed long-term use, with dirt on the floor and smoke-blackened walls. The hearth was greasy.

 (A) He feels guilty about having slept with Eldoria which perpetuated the demand for female prostitution. 
 (B) Even though he is a psycheye, he feels guilty about hunting down Sabrina York.

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
Blake Past stood up too. "No, not yet. I'll walk back to the sorority house with you."
She tossed her head, but the sadness in her tarn-blue eyes belied her hauteur. "If you wish," she said.
Blake Present watched them set out side by side toward the remembered halls of learning that showed in the distance. There had been other people present on the campus that afternoon, but as they had failed to register on Blake Past's mind, they did not exist for Blake Present. All that existed for Blake Present were the diminishing figures of the girl and the man, and the pain that was constricting his throat.
Wretchedly he turned away. As he did so he saw the three shadows lying at his feet and knew that his pursuers had at last caught up to him.
His first reaction when he faced them was amazement. His next reaction was shock. His third was fear.
His amazement resulted from recognition. One of the three women arrayed before him was Miss Stoddart, his boyhood Sunday-school teacher. Standing next to her in a familiar blue uniform was Officer Finch, the police woman who had maintained law and order in the collective elementary school he had attended. Standing next to Officer Finch was blond and chic Vera Velvetskin, whose picture he had seen on box after countless box of his mother's favorite detergent.
His shock resulted from the expressions on the three faces. Neither Miss Stoddart nor Officer Finch ever particularly liked him, but they had never particularly disliked him either. This Miss Stoddart and this Officer Finch disliked him, though. They hated him. They hated him so much that their hatred had thinned out their faces and darkened their eyes. More shocking yet, Vera Velvetskin, who had never existed save in some copywriter's mind, hated him too. In fact, judging from the greater thinness of her face and the more pronounced darkness of her eyes, she hated him even more than Miss Stoddart and Officer Finch did.
His fear resulted from the realization that his mind-world contained phenomena it had no right to contain—not if he was nearly as well-adjusted as he considered himself to be. The three women standing before him definitely were not memory-images. They were too vivid, for one thing. For another, they were aware of him. What were they, then? And what were they doing in his mind?
He asked the two questions aloud.
Three arms were raised and three forefingers were pointed accusingly at his chest. Three pairs of eyes burned darkly. "You ask us that?" Miss Stoddart said. "Callous creature who did a maiden's innocence affront!" said Officer Finch. "And sought sanctuary in ill-fitting robes of righteousness!" said Vera Velvetskin. The three faces moved together, blurred and seemed to blend into one. The three voices were raised in unison: "You know who we are, Nathan Blake.
You
know who we are!"
Blake stared at them open-mouthed. Then he turned and fled.

When he reached the alley, he ran until the chocoletto sector was far behind. The hill, steep like Aldebaran 12's, left him breathless at the crest. Ahead was a memory of Deneb 1 wasteland, a terrain he wished he hadn't remembered. His pursuers, barely visible in the rain, seemed closer. They had been trailing him for ten hours, but he couldn't face them. After resting, he crossed the detailed wasteland, following clear footprints. Mind-country was chaotic, blending times and places. Blake saw a boyhood meadow next to a wasteland, followed by a house from a later time, surrounded by diverse galactic landscapes. He stepped back, uneasy after seeing Sabrina York's name on the kitchen range. Systematically searching the house, he found no sign of her. At the stairs, he spotted his pursuers in the meadow and hesitated, surprised they were women. Controlling his fear, he left through the back door, following Sabrina's trail to a bench under an elm tree. Pausing by the trunk, memories of Deirdre's graduation and their past together overwhelmed him.
Deirdre insisted on Blake coming to her prom at nine. Blake, thirty-eight, refused, saying proms were for parents or the young man she talked to earlier. Deirdre argued she wasn't his daughter and he hadn't supported her education. Blake admitted buying her after her real father died was just to ease his conscience. Deirdre, about to leave for New Earth, pleaded with Blake, knowing he wouldn't come.
Blake Past stood up too. "No, not yet. I'll walk back to the sorority house with you."
She tossed her head, but the sadness in her tarn-blue eyes belied her hauteur. "If you wish," she said.
Blake Present watched them set out side by side toward the remembered halls of learning that showed in the distance. There had been other people present on the campus that afternoon, but as they had failed to register on Blake Past's mind, they did not exist for Blake Present. All that existed for Blake Present were the diminishing figures of the girl and the man, and the pain that was constricting his throat.
Wretchedly he turned away. As he did so he saw the three shadows lying at his feet and knew that his pursuers had at last caught up to him.
His first reaction when he faced them was amazement. His next reaction was shock. His third was fear.
His amazement resulted from recognition. One of the three women arrayed before him was Miss Stoddart, his boyhood Sunday-school teacher. Standing next to her in a familiar blue uniform was Officer Finch, the police woman who had maintained law and order in the collective elementary school he had attended. Standing next to Officer Finch was blond and chic Vera Velvetskin, whose picture he had seen on box after countless box of his mother's favorite detergent.
His shock resulted from the expressions on the three faces. Neither Miss Stoddart nor Officer Finch ever particularly liked him, but they had never particularly disliked him either. This Miss Stoddart and this Officer Finch disliked him, though. They hated him. They hated him so much that their hatred had thinned out their faces and darkened their eyes. More shocking yet, Vera Velvetskin, who had never existed save in some copywriter's mind, hated him too. In fact, judging from the greater thinness of her face and the more pronounced darkness of her eyes, she hated him even more than Miss Stoddart and Officer Finch did.
His fear resulted from the realization that his mind-world contained phenomena it had no right to contain—not if he was nearly as well-adjusted as he considered himself to be. She laughed. "I am here because I live here," she said.
He tried to assimilate the information, but could not. Perceiving his difficulty, the girl went on, "My parents indentured themselves to the Great Starway Cartel and were assigned to the rubber plantations of Dubhe 4. They died of yellow-water dysentery before their indenture ran out, and in accordance with Interstellar Law I was auctioned off along with the rest of their possessions. Eldoria bought me."
Five years as a roving psycheye had hardened Blake to commercial colonization practices; nevertheless, he found the present example of man's inhumanity to man sickening.
"How old are you?" Blake asked.
"Fourteen."
"And what are you going to be when you grow up?"
"Probably I shall be a psychiatrist. Eldoria is sending me to the mission school now, and afterward she is going to put me through an institute of higher learning. And when I come of age, she is going to give me my freedom."
"I see," Blake said. He indicated the book on her lap. "Homework?"
She shook her head. "In addition to my courses at the mission school, I am studying the humanities."
"Xenophon," Blake said. "And I suppose Plato too."
"And Homer and Virgil and Aeschylus and Euripides and all the rest of them. When I grow up I shall be a most well-educated person."
"I'm sure you will be," Blake said, looking at the arras.
"My name is Deirdre."
"Nathan," Blake said. "Nathan Blake."
"Eldoria will be arriving soon. I must go and prepare her dais."
She got up, parted the arras, and slipped into the next room. Shame flamed in Blake's cheeks, and for a moment he considered leaving; then he remembered Eldoria's dance, and he went right on sitting where he was.

read_agent_gists:
THE GIRL IN HIS MIND
By ROBERT F. YOUNG

Nathan Blake watched a chocoletto girl perform a dance hinting at the kylee sex ritual of Dubhe 7. Despite its expurgation, it was still provocative. Her G-string barely covered her, and Blake found her movements captivating. He asked if she was free and, after her dance, invited her to his table. She agreed for 3,000 quandoes, and he went to wait at her hut.

Upon arrival, Blake found a human girl reading Xenophon's Anabasis instead of the expected chocoletto hanger-on. She asked if he was there for Eldoria, and Blake nodded.

She laughed, "I live here. My parents died on the Dubhe 4 plantations, and I was auctioned off. Eldoria bought me." Blake, hardened by years as a psycheye, found the situation sickening. "How old are you?" "Fourteen. Eldoria is sending me to school and will free me when I come of age." Blake noted her studying humanities. "Xenophon?" "And more." She introduced herself as Deirdre, then left to prepare Eldoria's dais, leaving Blake feeling ashamed.

The girl returned, and the scent of incense filled the room. She sat sideways, and he noticed a saintly quality in her profile. He shifted uncomfortably as she read a book, relieved when Eldoria arrived and moved him to a larger, more luxurious room. Blake sat nervously as Eldoria undressed, noting the incense thickened around him. She reassured him about the girl, but he rose in the night, stepping over her sleeping form in the dawn.

When he reached the alley, he ran until the chocoletto sector was far behind. The hill, steep like Aldebaran 12's, left him breathless at the crest. Ahead was a memory of Deneb 1 wasteland, a terrain he wished he hadn't remembered. His pursuers, barely visible in the rain, seemed closer. They had been trailing him for ten hours, but he couldn't face them. After resting, he crossed the detailed wasteland, following clear footprints. Sabrina York, unaware of mind-tracker tricks, thought hiding in her tracker's mind was enough, unaware Blake had found her.

Mind-country was chaotic, blending times and places. Blake saw a boyhood meadow next to a wasteland, followed by a house from a later time, surrounded by diverse galactic landscapes. The house was flanked by a spaceport and a city-block, with a Martian waterway behind it. Sabrina's footsteps led to the front door, ajar. Blake entered, finding his father smoking and watching 3V, unaware of Blake's presence. In the kitchen, Blake saw his mother preparing a meal from twenty-three years ago. He noticed a name-plate on the range reading "Sabrina York."

He stepped back, uneasy after seeing Sabrina York's name on the kitchen range. Systematically searching the house, he found no sign of her. At the stairs, he spotted his pursuers in the meadow and hesitated, surprised they were women. Controlling his fear, he left through the back door, following Sabrina's trail to a bench under an elm tree. Pausing by the trunk, memories of Deirdre's graduation and their past together overwhelmed him.

Deirdre insisted on Blake coming to her prom at nine. Blake, thirty-eight, refused, saying proms were for parents or the young man she talked to earlier. Deirdre argued she wasn't his daughter and he hadn't supported her education. Blake admitted buying her after her real father died was just to ease his conscience. Deirdre, about to leave for New Earth, pleaded with Blake, knowing he wouldn't come.

Blake Past stood up. "I'll walk back to the sorority house with you."
She tossed her head, but her sadness showed in her eyes. "If you wish," she said.
Blake Present watched them walk away, feeling pain. He turned away, seeing three shadows at his feet.
Amazed, shocked, and then fearful, he faced Miss Stoddart, Officer Finch, and Vera Velvetskin. They hated him, their faces thin and eyes dark. He realized they weren't just memories but something else in his mind.
"Callous creature!" "Sought sanctuary!" they accused in unison. "You know who we are, Nathan Blake!"
Blake stared, then fled.

Man discovered he could create universes within his mind, a feat first achieved by psychologist Trevor when he entered a patient's unconscious memory. This led to the founding of a new psychological school and the development of a method to project oneself into past moments, known as "place-time." While entering another's mind-world required intensive study, the technique could be mastered with practice. These mind-countries were confusing, bearing no apparent relation to the objective universe, and their shapes and images varied based on individual interpretation.

It was inevitable that criminals would hide in their own mind-worlds until the statute of limitations ran out, leading to the creation of psyche-police and private psycheyes like Blake. In a first-ever case, a criminal, Sabrina York, hid in Blake's mind. Her entry into a sentimental memory of Blake's old office gave her away. Retiring to his quarters, Blake pursued her there. Her only advantage lost, she was at his mercy unless she found him first. Blake was troubled by how Sabrina knew about his old office and worried she might have created a superior mental being to aid her.

Blake created three malevolent super-images: Miss Stoddart, Officer Finch, and Vera Velvetskin. They followed him to a memory-image of Walden Pond, where they expressed delight, especially at Thoreau's shack. The scene shifted to an English park memorializing poets, with Burns' birthplace standing out vividly. Blake lingered despite feeling repugnance, remembering the cottage's details clearly.
He paused, seeing a man in the chair and a wine bottle on the table. The room showed long-term use, with dirt on the floor and smoke-blackened walls. The hearth was greasy.

Why doesn't Blake haggle with Eldoria about the price for her services?

 (A) He's afraid that if he angers her, she'll revert to the cannibalism of her forebears. 
 (B) He knows she needs the money to move out of her chocoletto hut. 
 (C) He has been making a lot of money as a private pyscheye and can afford the high price. 
 (D) He has never seen anyone like her, and after seeing her dance, he believes she's worth the price.

Answer with ONLY the exact text of the correct option, without the option letter, parentheses, or any extra words.
prediction: He has never seen anyone like her, and after seeing her dance, he believes she's worth the price.

query:
Why did the Tr'en leave Korvin's door unlocked and a weapon nearby?

 (A) They were so caught up trying to figure out  Korvin's answers that they became somewhat careless in guarding him. 
 (B) Their subconscious knew that Korvin was an insoluble problem. This same subconscious led them to provide resources for his escape so they wouldn't have to deal with him anymore. 
 (C) They were tired of the Ruler's dictatorship and intentionally provided resources for Korvin's escape in hopes that he would help them overthrow the Ruler.  
 (D) After their interview with Korvin, they determined he was wasteful and confusing, but not a threat. In order to avoid another confusing interaction with him, they simply provided resources for his escape.
references: Their subconscious knew that Korvin was an insoluble problem. This same subconscious led them to provide resources for his escape so they wouldn't have to deal with him anymore.

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
On the third day Korvin escaped.
It wasn't very difficult; he hadn't thought it would be. Even the most logical of thinking beings has a subconscious as well as a conscious mind, and one of the ways of dealing with an insoluble problem is to make the problem disappear. There were only two ways of doing that, and killing the problem's main focus was a little more complicated. That couldn't be done by the subconscious mind; the conscious had to intervene somewhere. And it couldn't.
Because that would mean recognizing, fully and consciously, that the problem
was
insoluble. And the Tr'en weren't capable of that sort of thinking.
Korvin thanked his lucky stars that their genius had been restricted to the physical and mathematical. Any insight at all into the mental sciences would have given them the key to his existence, and his entire plan, within seconds.
But, then, it was lack of that insight that had called for this particular plan. That, and the political structure of the Tr'en.
The same lack of insight let the Tr'en subconscious work on his escape without any annoying distractions in the way of deep reflection. Someone left a door unlocked and a weapon nearby—all quite intent, Korvin was sure. Getting to the ship was a little more complicated, but presented no new problems; he was airborne, and then space-borne, inside of a few hours after leaving the cell.
He set his course, relaxed, and cleared his mind. He had no psionic talents, but the men at Earth Central did; he couldn't receive messages, but he could send them. He sent one now.
Mission accomplished; the Tr'en aren't about to come marauding out into space too soon. They've been given food for thought—nice indigestible food that's going to stick in their craws until they finally manage to digest it. But they can't digest it and stay what they are; you've got to be democratic, to some extent, to understand the idea. What keeps us obeying laws we ourselves make? What keeps us obeying laws that make things inconvenient for us? Sheer self-interest, of course—but try to make a Tr'en see it!

read_agent_gists:
LOST IN TRANSLATION
By LARRY M. HARRIS
Korvin, in a Tr'en cell, reflects on his isolation and the Tr'en's efficiency. He wishes for basic entertainment and company. Every night, he receives language lessons while unconscious. Bored and ready to converse, the cell door opens, revealing a tall, slightly green Tr'en visitor.

The Tr'en, Didyak, regarded Korvin narrowly and identified him. Korvin, a captive, responded formally and noted Didyak's small weapons. Korvin asked about his purpose, learning he was ordered by the Ruler to talk with Korvin.

Korvin asked Didyak why the Ruler didn't come to him. Didyak, slightly uncomfortable, replied that Korvin was to go to the Ruler as commanded. Korvin, sighing, agreed to obey, though he planned to eliminate the Tr'en threat. The Ruler's room was overly brown, with the Ruler tapping a table, staring at Korvin and his impassive guards. Korvin answered questions about his race, name, sex, and appearance, as the Tr'en valued clarity and logic.

Korvin answered, "Some men are over eight feet tall, and others under four feet. There are also humans never more than a foot and a half tall, called children. 'Approximately?' the Ruler growled, demanding precision. 'We are scientific men.' Korvin nodded, admitting his race was more approximate. The Ruler accused Korvin of lying about crashing his ship, which Korvin admitted was true. The Ruler then brought in a lie-detector machine, marveling at the Tr'en's scientific advancements while fearing their menace. Korvin, hoping the machine was adjusted correctly, allowed technicians to strap him in without resistance.

"We shall test the machine," the Ruler said. "In what room are you?" "In the Room of the Ruler," Korvin said. "Are you standing or sitting?" "I am sitting," Korvin said. "Are you a chulad?" "I am not," he said. The Ruler nodded on receiving a signal from his technicians. "You will tell an untruth now. Are you standing or sitting?" "I am standing," Korvin said. The technicians signaled again. "The machine has been adjusted satisfactorily to your physiology," the Ruler announced. "Why did you land your ship on this planet?" "My job required it," Korvin said. The Ruler nodded. "Your job is to crash your ship. Was the crash intentional?" "Yes," he said. "Was your job ended when the ship crashed?" The Tr'en word meant "disposed of for all time."

"No," he said.
"What else does your job entail?" the Ruler asked.
Korvin replied, "Staying alive."
The Ruler roared, "Do not waste time with the obvious! Answer correctly!"
"I have told the truth," Korvin said.
"But it is not the truth we want," the Ruler said.
Korvin shrugged. "I replied to your question. Surely the truth is the truth?"
The Ruler stopped, "You try to confuse the Ruler. We have experts in logic who will advise."
Korvin's guards left quickly.
The Ruler said, "You cannot trick us. Such attempts will get you nowhere."
Korvin hoped so.

Experts in logic arrived, informing Korvin that paradoxes like the barber who shaves himself or the club of secretaries were merely primer material for them. "We treat them mathematically," an expert explained, "though you wouldn't understand. You just need to know we're not confused." Korvin agreed, leading the expert to suspect a different plan. When asked about his plan, Korvin truthfully stated it was to answer questions logically. The experts, trusting the lie detector, concluded Korvin must be a traitor aiding their conquest of Earth. Korvin clarified Earth had no inherent name, only names people gave it.

"Yet you call it Earth?" the Ruler said.
"I do, for convenience," Korvin said.
"Do you know its location?" the Ruler asked.
"Not with exactitude," Korvin replied.
"But you can find it again," the Ruler said.
"I can," Korvin said.
"And you will tell us about it?" the Ruler asked.
"I will, so far as I am able," Korvin said.
"We must first know of the manner of decision on this planet. Is your planet joined with others in a government or does it exist alone?" the Ruler asked.
Korvin nearly smiled. "Both," he said.
"Then the government which reigns over several planets is supreme," the Ruler said.
"It is," Korvin said.
"Who is it that governs?" the Ruler said.
"The answer to that question cannot be given to you," Korvin said.
"Explain this governor to

Korvin tried to look grateful. "What is your government?" he asked.

The Ruler signaled a green being who stepped forward and explained, "Our government ensures uniformity and swift action. The Ruler commands, and subjects obey."

"You define yours," the Ruler instructed Korvin.

Korvin shook his head. "If you must, I'll try. But you won't understand. We have no single governor, yet everyone is a governor. Many decisions bind us, but we're not forced to accept them."

"Do you act against your own interests?"

Korvin shrugged, "Not knowingly." The Ruler glanced at the lie-detector technicians, who confirmed Korvin's truth. "I told you you wouldn't understand," he said. The Ruler accused a defect in Korvin's explanation. "My explanation is exact," Korvin replied. "Let's try something else," the Ruler suggested, questioning Korvin's lack of a single ruler. "We have democracy," Korvin calmly stated. An expert argued this was impossible without force, but Korvin insisted. The expert accused Korvin of lying, but the lie-detector confirmed Korvin's truth. Korvin hoped the experts wouldn't take long to realize the machine wasn't faulty. It took three days, but Korvin faced constant attention from puzzled experts.

On the third day, Korvin escaped easily. The Tr'en's subconscious helped him without deep reflection due to their lack of insight into mental sciences. He thanked his luck that their genius was limited to physical and mathematical fields. Escaping to the ship was more complex but manageable. He relaxed, set his course, and sent a message to Earth Central, declaring his mission accomplished. The Tr'en would ponder his actions, but their rigid structure would prevent them from fully understanding his point.

With one government and one language, they lacked translation skills. Too efficient physically, they ignored mental sciences, thus no insight into minds—no translation. Damn it, I wish I were home. I'm bored stiff! THE END

It wasn't very difficult; he hadn't thought it would be. Even the most logical of thinking beings has a subconscious as well as a conscious mind, and one of the ways of dealing with an insoluble problem is to make the problem disappear. There were only two ways of doing that, and killing the problem's main focus was a little more complicated. That couldn't be done by the subconscious mind; the conscious had to intervene somewhere. And it couldn't.
Because that would mean recognizing, fully and consciously, that the problem
was
insoluble. And the Tr'en weren't capable of that sort of thinking.
Korvin thanked his lucky stars that their genius had been restricted to the physical and mathematical. Any insight at all into the mental sciences would have given them the key to his existence, and his entire plan, within seconds.
But, then, it was lack of that insight that had called for this particular plan. That, and the political structure of the Tr'en.
The same lack of insight let the Tr'en subconscious work on his escape without any annoying distractions in the way of deep reflection. Someone left a door unlocked and a weapon nearby—all quite intent, Korvin was sure. Getting to the ship was a little more complicated, but presented no new problems; he was airborne, and then space-borne, inside of a few hours after leaving the cell.
He set his course, relaxed, and cleared his mind. He had no psionic talents, but the men at Earth Central did; he couldn't receive messages, but he could send them. He sent one now.
Mission accomplished; the Tr'en aren't about to come marauding out into space too soon. They've been given food for thought—nice indigestible food that's going to stick in their craws until they finally manage to digest it. But they can't digest it and stay what they are; you've got to be democratic, to some extent, to understand the idea. What keeps us obeying laws we ourselves make? What keeps us obeying laws that make things inconvenient for us? Sheer self-interest, of course—but try to make a Tr'en see it!
With one government and one language, they lacked translation skills. Too efficient physically, they ignored mental sciences, thus no insight into minds—no translation. Damn it, I wish I were home. I'm bored stiff! THE END

Why did the Tr'en leave Korvin's door unlocked and a weapon nearby?

 (A) They were so caught up trying to figure out  Korvin's answers that they became somewhat careless in guarding him. 
 (B) Their subconscious knew that Korvin was an insoluble problem. This same subconscious led them to provide resources for his escape so they wouldn't have to deal with him anymore. 
 (C) They were tired of the Ruler's dictatorship and intentionally provided resources for Korvin's escape in hopes that he would help them overthrow the Ruler.  
 (D) After their interview with Korvin, they determined he was wasteful and confusing, but not a threat. In order to avoid another confusing interaction with him, they simply provided resources for his escape.

Answer with ONLY the exact text of the correct option, without the option letter, parentheses, or any extra words.

 (A) It means that the Tr'en put Korvin under drug hypnosis while they taught him their language.

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
LOST
IN
TRANSLATION
By
LARRY M.
HARRIS
In language translation, you may get a literally accurate word-for-word translation ... but miss the meaning entirely. And in space-type translation ... the effect may be the same!
Illustrated by Schoenherr
The cell had been put together more efficiently than any Korvin had ever been in. But that was only natural, he told himself sadly; the Tr'en were an efficient people. All the preliminary reports had agreed on that; their efficiency, as a matter of fact, was what had made Korvin's arrival a necessity. They were well into the atomic era, and were on the verge of developing space travel. Before long they'd be settling the other planets of their system, and then the nearer stars. Faster-than-light travel couldn't be far away, for the magnificently efficient physical scientists of the Tr'en—and that would mean, in the ordinary course of events, an invitation to join the Comity of Planets.
An invitation, the Comity was sure, which the Tr'en would not accept.
Korvin stretched out on the cell's single bunk, a rigid affair which was hardly meant for comfort, and sighed. He'd had three days of isolation, with nothing to do but explore the resources of his own mind. He'd tried some of the ancient Rhine experiments, but that was no good; he still didn't show any particular psi talents. He couldn't unlock the cell door with his unaided mind; he couldn't even alter the probability of a single dust-mote's Brownian path through the somewhat smelly air. Nor could he disappear from his cell and appear, as if by magic, several miles away near the slightly-damaged hulk of his ship, to the wonder and amazement of his Tr'en captors.
He could do, as a matter of fact, precisely nothing. He wished quietly that the Tr'en had seen fit to give him a pack of cards, or a book, or even a folder of tourist pictures. The Wonders of Tr'en, according to all the advance reports, were likely to be pretty boring, but they'd have been better than nothing.
In any decently-run jail, he told himself with indignation, there would at least have been other prisoners to talk to. But on Tr'en Korvin was all alone.
True, every night the guards came in and gave him a concentrated lesson in the local language, but Korvin failed to get much pleasure out of that, being unconscious at the time. But now he was equipped to discuss almost anything from philosophy to plumbing, but there was nobody to discuss it with. He changed position on the bunk and stared at the walls. The Tr'en were efficient; there weren't even any imperfections in the smooth surface to distract him.
He wasn't tired and he wasn't hungry; his captors had left him with a full stock of food concentrates.
But he was almightily bored, and about ready to tell anything to anyone, just for the chance at a little conversation.
As he reached this dismal conclusion, the cell door opened. Korvin got up off the bunk in a hurry and spun around to face his visitor.
The Tr'en was tall, and slightly green.
He looked, as all the Tr'en did, vaguely humanoid—that is, if you don't bother to examine him closely. Life in the universe appeared to be rigidly limited to humanoid types on oxygen planets; Korvin didn't know why, and neither did anybody else. There were a lot of theories, but none that accounted for all the facts satisfactorily. Korvin really didn't care about it; it was none of his business.

read_agent_gists:
LOST IN TRANSLATION
By LARRY M. HARRIS
Korvin, in a Tr'en cell, reflects on his isolation and the Tr'en's efficiency. He wishes for basic entertainment and company. Every night, he receives language lessons while unconscious. Bored and ready to converse, the cell door opens, revealing a tall, green Tr'en visitor.

The Tr'en, Didyak, regarded Korvin narrowly and identified him. Korvin, a captive, responded formally and noted Didyak's small weapons. Korvin asked about his purpose, learning he was ordered by the Ruler to talk with Korvin.

It wasn't "talk"; Didyak meant "gain peaceful, vocal information." Korvin filed it away. "Why didn't the Ruler come to me?" Korvin asked.

"The Ruler is the Ruler," Didyak said, slightly uncomfortable. "You go to him. His command."

Korvin shrugged, sighed, and smoothed his hair. "I obey the Ruler's command," he said—another ritual. Everyone obeyed the Ruler. If not, no second chance.

But Korvin meant it. He would obey the Ruler and remove the Tr'en threat from the galaxy.

The Room of the Ruler was large, square, and brown. The Ruler, over seven feet tall, sat tapping his fingers, staring at Korvin and his guards. The guards stood impassively.

Korvin wasn't escaping, pleading, or defying. He was answering questions.

The Ruler started with Kor

Korvin watched as a large, metallic lie-detector machine was wheeled into the room by two technicians. He felt a mix of awe and dread, knowing the Tr'en's advanced technology could be dangerous. As he was strapped into the machine, he hoped it was adjusted correctly, as lying could mean death. The Ruler began the test, asking Korvin if he was standing or sitting, to which Korvin truthfully replied he was sitting. Then, the Ruler asked if Korvin was a chulad, a native pet. Korvin denied it, and the Ruler prepared to ask a lie to test the machine.

"I am standing," Korvin said.
The Ruler, frowning, announced the machine was adjusted to Korvin's physiology and questioning would continue. Korvin felt the test was insufficient but trusted the Tr'en's expertise. The Ruler asked why Korvin landed his ship on the planet. Korvin replied it was job-related. The Ruler noted his job was to crash the ship wastefully and asked if the crash was intentional. Korvin confirmed it was. The Ruler then asked if his job ended with the crash. Korvin said no. When asked what else his job entailed, Korvin said staying alive. The Ruler roared, demanding truthful answers, not tricks. Korvin insisted he told the truth. The Ruler, confused, called for logic experts. Korvin's guards left quickly.

The Ruler told Korvin, "You can't trick us." Korvin hoped so. Logic experts arrived, dismissing paradoxes as primer material for the Tr'en. When Korvin said it was "good," the expert misunderstood, thinking Korvin's plan was to answer questions truthfully and logically. The lie detector confirmed Korvin's truthfulness, leading the experts to conclude he was a traitor aiding their conquest.

Korvin, strapped to a machine, dodged questions about Earth’s name and location, claiming it had no name and he knew it vaguely but could find it. When asked about Earth’s government, he said it was both joined with others and alone. The Ruler pressed for details, but Korvin avoided revealing who governed, insisting it was obvious a government had a governor. Frustrated, the Ruler demanded an explanation of this governor.

Korvin shrugged, "The explanation can't be given." The Ruler consulted his experts, then asked Korvin if he was unable to describe the government. Korvin said he could but wouldn't suffer consequences for describing it. The Ruler, now slightly puzzled, accused Korvin of trying to confuse them. Korvin denied it and asked to ask a question. The Ruler agreed. Korvin asked, "What is your government?" A green Tr'en explained their government was a logical form where the Ruler orders and subjects obey, ensuring uniformity and swift action.

"You have heard our government," the Ruler said. "Now, define yours."  
Korvin shook his head. "If you insist, I'll try. But you won't understand."  
The Ruler frowned. "We shall. Begin. Who governs you?"  
"None," Korvin said.  
"But you are governed?"  
Korvin nodded. "Yes."  
"Then there is a governor," the Ruler insisted.  
"True," Korvin said. "But everyone is the governor."  
"Then there is no government," the Ruler said. "No single decision."  
"No," Korvin said. "Many decisions binding on all."  
"Who makes them binding?" the Ruler asked. "Who forces you to accept these decisions?"  
"Many are unfavorable," Korvin said. "But we are not forced to accept them."  
"Do you act against your own interests?"  
K

Korvin calmly stated they had no need for a ruler, explaining their government was democracy, where people rule themselves. An expert dismissed this as impossible without force, but Korvin insisted. The expert accused Korvin of lying, and the technicians argued about a faulty machine. Korvin grew homesick and predicted a long wait. However, he escaped in three days when the experts were distracted, exploiting their inability to grasp mental sciences. His escape was made easier by their subconscious actions, leading him to a ship and into space within hours.

He set his course, relaxed, and sent a message. Mission accomplished; the Tr'en would be occupied with indigestible thoughts, unable to digest them without becoming more democratic. They lacked the mental sciences for translation and insight. He wished he were home, bored stiff. THE END

query:
How was Korvin able to avoid disclosing the true intent of his mission under the lie detector questioning? 

 (A) While he was strapped down in the lie-detector, Korvin subtly switched the wire that indicated a truth with the one that indicated a lie. 
 (B) Korvin said truths that literally answered the Tr'en's questions but evaded the intent behind their questions. .
 (C) The Tr'en hadn't tested the lie-detector extensively enough and the machine was faulty. 
 (D) Even with the Tr'en's language lessons, Korvin could only to speak in very simple terms and was unable to answer the Ruler's questions at the depth the Ruler was expecting.
references: Korvin said truths that literally answered the Tr'en's questions but evaded the intent behind their questions. .

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
"You have heard our government defined," the Ruler said. "Now, you will define yours for us."
Korvin shook his head. "If you insist," he said, "I'll try it. But you won't understand it."
The Ruler frowned. "We shall understand," he said. "Begin. Who governs you?"
"None," Korvin said.
"But you are governed?"
Korvin nodded. "Yes."
"Then there is a governor," the Ruler insisted.
"True," Korvin said. "But everyone is the governor."
"Then there is no government," the Ruler said. "There is no single decision."
"No," Korvin said equably, "there are many decisions binding on all."
"Who makes them binding?" the Ruler asked. "Who forces you to accept these decisions? Some of them must be unfavorable to some beings?"
"Many of them are unfavorable," Korvin said. "But we are not forced to accept them."
"Do you act against your own interests?"
Korvin shrugged. "Not knowingly," he said. The Ruler flashed a look at the technicians handling the lie-detector. Korvin turned to see their expression. They needed no words; the lie-detector was telling them, perfectly obviously, that he was speaking the truth. But the truth wasn't making any sense. "I told you you wouldn't understand it," he said.
"It is a defect in your explanation," the Ruler almost snarled.
"My explanation is as exact as it can be," he said.
The Ruler breathed gustily. "Let us try something else," he said. "Everyone is the governor. Do you share a single mind? A racial mind has been theorized, though we have met with no examples—"
"Neither have we," Korvin said. "We are all individuals, like yourselves."
"But with no single ruler to form policy, to make decisions—"

"It is," Korvin confirmed. "Who governs?" the Ruler asked. "That can't be given," Korvin said. "Any government has a governor," the Ruler snapped

Korvin shrugged, "The explanation cannot be given to you." The Ruler consulted his experts, then asked Korvin if he was unable to describe the government or would suffer consequences if he did. Korvin denied both, noting the Tr'en were becoming puzzled. The Ruler accused Korvin of trying to confuse them, but Korvin requested to ask a question instead. The Ruler agreed, and Korvin asked, "What is your government?" The Ruler's advisor explained their government as a system where the Ruler orders and all subjects obey, ensuring uniformity and swift action.

"You have heard our government," the Ruler said. "Now, define yours."  
Korvin nodded. "If you insist, I'll try. But you won't understand."  
The Ruler frowned. "We shall. Begin. Who governs you?"  
"None," Korvin said.  
"But you are governed?"  
"Yes," Korvin nodded. "But everyone is the governor."  
"Then there is no government," the Ruler said. "No single decision."  
"No," Korvin said. "Many decisions binding on all."  
"Who makes them binding?"  
"Many are unfavorable," Korvin said. "But we're not forced to accept them."  
"Do you act against your own interests?"  
"Not knowingly," Korvin shrugged. The lie-detector confirmed his truth. "I told you, you wouldn't understand."  
"It's a defect in your explanation," the Ruler snarled

Korvin calmly stated they had no need for a ruler, explaining their government was democracy, where people rule themselves. An expert dismissed this as impossible without force, but Korvin insisted. The expert accused Korvin of lying, and the technicians argued about a faulty machine. Korvin grew homesick and predicted a long wait. However, he escaped in three days when the experts were distracted, exploiting their inability to grasp mental sciences. His escape was made easier by their subconscious actions, leading to a quick departure from the planet.

He set his course, relaxed, and sent a message. Mission accomplished; the Tr'en would be occupied with indigestible thoughts, unable to digest them without becoming more democratic. They lacked the mental sciences for translation and insight. He wished he were home, bored stiff. THE END

user_prompt:
Text:
LOST IN TRANSLATION
By LARRY M. HARRIS
Korvin, in a Tr'en cell, reflects on his isolation and the Tr'en's efficiency. He wishes for basic entertainment and company. Every night, he receives language lessons while unconscious. Bored and ready to converse, the cell door opens, revealing a tall, green Tr'en visitor.
The Tr'en, Didyak, regarded Korvin narrowly and identified him. Korvin, a captive, responded formally and noted Didyak's small weapons. Korvin asked about his purpose, learning he was ordered by the Ruler to talk with Korvin.
It wasn't "talk"; Didyak meant "gain peaceful, vocal information." Korvin filed it away. "Why didn't the Ruler come to me?" Korvin asked.

"The Ruler is the Ruler," Didyak said, slightly uncomfortable. "You go to him. His command."

Korvin shrugged, sighed, and smoothed his hair. "I obey the Ruler's command," he said—another ritual. Everyone obeyed the Ruler. If not, no second chance.

But Korvin meant it. He would obey the Ruler and remove the Tr'en threat from the galaxy.

The Room of the Ruler was large, square, and brown. The Ruler, over seven feet tall, sat tapping his fingers, staring at Korvin and his guards. Korvin wasn't escaping, pleading, or defying. He was answering questions.

The Ruler started with Korvin's race, name, sex
Korvin watched as a large, metallic lie-detector machine was wheeled into the room by two technicians. He felt a mix of awe and dread, knowing the Tr'en's advanced technology could be dangerous. As he was strapped into the machine, he hoped it was adjusted correctly, as lying could mean death. The Ruler began the test, asking Korvin if he was standing or sitting, to which Korvin truthfully replied he was sitting. Then, the Ruler asked if Korvin was a chulad, a native pet. Korvin denied it, and the Ruler prepared to ask a lie to test the machine.
Korvin stood as the Ruler, satisfied with the machine's adjustments, began questioning him. Despite the test seeming insufficient, Korvin trusted the Tr'en's expertise. When asked why he landed his ship, Korvin replied it was job-related. The Ruler, however, interpreted "job" as a directive to crash the ship intentionally. Korvin confirmed the crash was intentional but denied his job was over. When asked what else his job entailed, he said staying alive, prompting the Ruler to demand more specific answers. The Ruler called in logic experts, and Korvin's guards left.
The Ruler told Korvin, "You can't trick us." Korvin hoped so. Logic experts arrived, explaining they couldn't be confused by paradoxes. Korvin said, "Good." The expert, puzzled, asked about Korvin's plan. Korvin replied, "To answer truthfully and logically." The expert, now awed, accused Korvin of being a traitor aiding their conquest.
Korvin was glad it wasn't a question. It was wrong, though. The Ruler asked if his planet was Earth. "Sometimes," Korvin said. "It has other names?" "It has no name," Korvin said truthfully. "Yet you call it Earth?" "For convenience," Korvin replied. "Do you know its location?" "Not exactly," Korvin said. "But you can find it again?" "I can," Korvin said. "And you will tell us about it?" "I will," Korvin said. "We must know about weapons and plans. Is your planet joined with others in a government or alone?" "Both," Korvin said. An expert theorized about decision-making systems. "It is," Korvin confirmed. "Who governs?" the Ruler asked. "That can't be given," Korvin said. "Any government has a governor," the Ruler snapped
Korvin shrugged, "The explanation cannot be given to you." The Ruler consulted his experts, then asked Korvin if he was unable to describe the government or would suffer consequences if he did. Korvin denied both, noting the Tr'en were becoming puzzled. The Ruler accused Korvin of trying to confuse them, but Korvin requested to ask a question instead. The Ruler agreed, and Korvin asked, "What is your government?" The Ruler's advisor explained their government as a system where the Ruler orders and all subjects obey, ensuring uniformity and swift action.
"You have heard our government defined," the Ruler said. "Now, you will define yours for us."
Korvin shook his head. "If you insist," he said, "I'll try it. But you won't understand it."
The Ruler frowned. "We shall understand," he said. "Begin. Who governs you?"
"None," Korvin said.
"But you are governed?"
Korvin nodded. "Yes."
"Then there is a governor," the Ruler insisted.
"True," Korvin said. "But everyone is the governor."
"Then there is no government," the Ruler said. "There is no single decision."
"No," Korvin said equably, "there are many decisions binding on all."
"Who makes them binding?" the Ruler asked. "Who forces you to accept these decisions? Some of them must be unfavorable to some beings?"
"Many of them are unfavorable," Korvin said. "But we are not forced to accept them."
"Do you act against your own interests?"
Korvin shrugged. "Not knowingly," he said. The Ruler flashed a look at the technicians handling the lie-detector. Korvin turned to see their expression. They needed no words; the lie-detector was telling them, perfectly obviously, that he was speaking the truth. But the truth wasn't making any sense. "I told you you wouldn't understand it," he said.
"It is a defect in your explanation," the Ruler almost snarled.
"My explanation is as exact as it can be," he said.
The Ruler breathed gustily. "Let us try something else," he said. "Everyone is the governor. Do you share a single mind? A racial mind has been theorized, though we have met with no examples—"
"Neither have we," Korvin said. "We are all individuals, like yourselves."
"But with no single ruler to form policy, to make decisions—"
Korvin calmly stated they had no need for a ruler, explaining their government was democracy, where people rule themselves. An expert dismissed this as impossible without force, but Korvin insisted. The expert accused Korvin of lying, and the technicians argued about a faulty machine. Korvin grew homesick and predicted a long wait. However, he escaped in three days when the experts were distracted, exploiting their inability to grasp mental sciences. His escape was made easier by their subconscious actions, leading to a quick departure from the planet.
He set his course, relaxed, and sent a message. Mission accomplished; the Tr'en would be occupied with indigestible thoughts, unable to digest them without becoming more democratic. They lacked the mental sciences for translation and insight. He wished he were home, bored stiff. THE END

How was Korvin able to avoid disclosing the true intent of his mission under the lie detector questioning? 

 (A) While he was strapped down in the lie-detector, Korvin subtly switched the wire that indicated a truth with the one that indicated a lie. 
 (B) Korvin said truths that literally answered the Tr'en's questions but evaded the intent behind their questions. .
 (C) The Tr'en hadn't tested the lie-detector extensively enough and the machine was faulty. 
 (D) Even with the Tr'en's language lessons, Korvin could only to speak in very simple terms and was unable to answer the Ruler's questions at the depth the Ruler was expecting.

Answer with ONLY the exact text of the correct option, without the option letter, parentheses, or any extra words.
prediction: Korvin said truths that literally answered the Tr'en's questions but evaded the intent behind their questions.

query:
What is the most likely reason for Korvin's solitude in jail?

 (A) Solitary confinement was part of Korvin's punishment. 
 (B) There weren't any other prisoners in the jail because virtually all of the Tr'en obey the Ruler. Those who don't obey are executed.
 (C) The Tr'en didn't want Korvin to interact with the other Tr'en prisoners because there was a chance that together they might incite an uprising. 
 (D) The Tr'en are so logical and mathematic that they don't see the need for social interaction.
references: There weren't any other prisoners in the jail because virtually all of the Tr'en obey the Ruler. Those who don't obey are executed.

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
LOST
IN
TRANSLATION
By
LARRY M.
HARRIS
In language translation, you may get a literally accurate word-for-word translation ... but miss the meaning entirely. And in space-type translation ... the effect may be the same!
Illustrated by Schoenherr
The cell had been put together more efficiently than any Korvin had ever been in. But that was only natural, he told himself sadly; the Tr'en were an efficient people. All the preliminary reports had agreed on that; their efficiency, as a matter of fact, was what had made Korvin's arrival a necessity. They were well into the atomic era, and were on the verge of developing space travel. Before long they'd be settling the other planets of their system, and then the nearer stars. Faster-than-light travel couldn't be far away, for the magnificently efficient physical scientists of the Tr'en—and that would mean, in the ordinary course of events, an invitation to join the Comity of Planets.
An invitation, the Comity was sure, which the Tr'en would not accept.
Korvin stretched out on the cell's single bunk, a rigid affair which was hardly meant for comfort, and sighed. He'd had three days of isolation, with nothing to do but explore the resources of his own mind. He'd tried some of the ancient Rhine experiments, but that was no good; he still didn't show any particular psi talents. He couldn't unlock the cell door with his unaided mind; he couldn't even alter the probability of a single dust-mote's Brownian path through the somewhat smelly air. Nor could he disappear from his cell and appear, as if by magic, several miles away near the slightly-damaged hulk of his ship, to the wonder and amazement of his Tr'en captors.
He could do, as a matter of fact, precisely nothing. He wished quietly that the Tr'en had seen fit to give him a pack of cards, or a book, or even a folder of tourist pictures. The Wonders of Tr'en, according to all the advance reports, were likely to be pretty boring, but they'd have been better than nothing.
In any decently-run jail, he told himself with indignation, there would at least have been other prisoners to talk to. But on Tr'en Korvin was all alone.
True, every night the guards came in and gave him a concentrated lesson in the local language, but Korvin failed to get much pleasure out of that, being unconscious at the time. But now he was equipped to discuss almost anything from philosophy to plumbing, but there was nobody to discuss it with. He changed position on the bunk and stared at the walls. The Tr'en were efficient; there weren't even any imperfections in the smooth surface to distract him.
He wasn't tired and he wasn't hungry; his captors had left him with a full stock of food concentrates.
But he was almightily bored, and about ready to tell anything to anyone, just for the chance at a little conversation.
As he reached this dismal conclusion, the cell door opened. Korvin got up off the bunk in a hurry and spun around to face his visitor.
The Tr'en was tall, and slightly green.
He looked, as all the Tr'en did, vaguely humanoid—that is, if you don't bother to examine him closely. Life in the universe appeared to be rigidly limited to humanoid types on oxygen planets; Korvin didn't know why, and neither did anybody else. There were a lot of theories, but none that accounted for all the facts satisfactorily. Korvin really didn't care about it; it was none of his business.

query:
Why does the Tr'en's logic fail them?

 (A) Because the lie-detector was faulty and Korvin gave them an insoluble paradox.  
 (B) Because it's too mathematical and doesn't account for motivations, emotions, and what's left unsaid. 
 (C) Because Korvin switched the wires on the lie-detector and gave the Tr'en an insoluble paradox. 
 (D) Because it's tightly controlled by the Ruler who is quite simple minded.
references: Because it's too mathematical and doesn't account for motivations, emotions, and what's left unsaid.

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
"You have heard our government defined," the Ruler said. "Now, you will define yours for us."
Korvin shook his head. "If you insist," he said, "I'll try it. But you won't understand it."
The Ruler frowned. "We shall understand," he said. "Begin. Who governs you?"
"None," Korvin said.
"But you are governed?"
Korvin nodded. "Yes."
"Then there is a governor," the Ruler insisted.
"True," Korvin said. "But everyone is the governor."
"Then there is no government," the Ruler said. "There is no single decision."
"No," Korvin said equably, "there are many decisions binding on all."
"Who makes them binding?" the Ruler asked. "Who forces you to accept these decisions? Some of them must be unfavorable to some beings?"
"Many of them are unfavorable," Korvin said. "But we are not forced to accept them."
"Do you act against your own interests?"
Korvin shrugged. "Not knowingly," he said. The Ruler flashed a look at the technicians handling the lie-detector. Korvin turned to see their expression. They needed no words; the lie-detector was telling them, perfectly obviously, that he was speaking the truth. But the truth wasn't making any sense. "I told you you wouldn't understand it," he said.
"It is a defect in your explanation," the Ruler almost snarled.
"My explanation is as exact as it can be," he said.
The Ruler breathed gustily. "Let us try something else," he said. "Everyone is the governor. Do you share a single mind? A racial mind has been theorized, though we have met with no examples—"
"Neither have we," Korvin said. "We are all individuals, like yourselves."
"But with no single ruler to form policy, to make decisions—"

read_agent_gists:
LOST IN TRANSLATION
By LARRY M. HARRIS
Korvin, in a Tr'en cell, reflects on his isolation and the Tr'en's efficiency. He wishes for basic entertainment and company. Every night, he receives language lessons while unconscious. Bored and ready to converse, the cell door opens, revealing a tall, green Tr'en visitor.

The Tr'en, Didyak, regarded Korvin narrowly and identified him. Korvin, a captive, responded formally and noted Didyak's small weapons. Korvin asked about his purpose, learning he was ordered by the Ruler to talk with Korvin.

It wasn't "talk"; Didyak meant "gain peaceful, vocal information." Korvin filed it away. "Why didn't the Ruler come to me?" Korvin asked.

"The Ruler is the Ruler," Didyak said, slightly uncomfortable. "You go to him. His command."

Korvin shrugged, sighed, and smoothed his hair. "I obey the Ruler's command," he said—another ritual. Everyone obeyed the Ruler. If not, no second chance.

But Korvin meant it. He would obey the Ruler and remove the Tr'en threat from the galaxy.

The Room of the Ruler was large, square, and brown. The Ruler, over seven feet tall, sat tapping his fingers, staring at Korvin and his guards. Korvin wasn't escaping, pleading, or defying. He was answering questions.

The Ruler started with Korvin's race, name, sex

Korvin watched as a large, metallic lie-detector machine was wheeled into the room by two technicians. He felt a mix of awe and dread, knowing the Tr'en's advanced technology could be dangerous. As he was strapped into the machine, he hoped it was adjusted correctly, as lying could mean death. The Ruler began the test, asking Korvin if he was standing or sitting, to which Korvin truthfully replied he was sitting. Then, the Ruler asked if Korvin was a chulad, a native pet. Korvin denied it, and the Ruler prepared to ask a lie to test the machine.

Korvin stood as the Ruler, satisfied with the machine's adjustments, began questioning him. Despite the test seeming insufficient, Korvin trusted the Tr'en's expertise. When asked why he landed his ship, Korvin replied it was job-related. The Ruler, however, interpreted "job" as a directive to crash the ship intentionally. Korvin confirmed the crash was intentional but denied his job was over. When asked what else his job entailed, he said staying alive, prompting the Ruler to demand more specific answers. The Ruler called in logic experts, and Korvin's guards left.

The Ruler told Korvin, "You can't trick us." Korvin hoped so. Logic experts arrived, explaining they couldn't be confused by paradoxes. Korvin said, "Good." The expert, puzzled, asked about Korvin's plan. Korvin replied, "To answer truthfully and logically." The expert, now awed, accused Korvin of being a traitor aiding their conquest.

Korvin was glad it wasn't a question. It was wrong, though. The Ruler asked if his planet was Earth. "Sometimes," Korvin said. "It has other names?" "It has no name," Korvin said truthfully. "Yet you call it Earth?" "For convenience," Korvin replied. "Do you know its location?" "Not exactly," Korvin said. "But you can find it again?" "I can," Korvin said. "And you will tell us about it?" "I will," Korvin said. "We must know about weapons and plans. Is your planet joined with others in a government or alone?" "Both," Korvin said. An expert theorized about decision-making systems. "It is," Korvin confirmed. "Who governs?" the Ruler asked. "That can't be given," Korvin said. "Any government has a governor," the Ruler snapped

Korvin shrugged, "The explanation cannot be given to you." The Ruler consulted his experts, then asked Korvin if he was unable to describe the government or would suffer consequences if he did. Korvin denied both, noting the Tr'en were becoming puzzled. The Ruler accused Korvin of trying to confuse them, but Korvin requested to ask a question instead. The Ruler agreed, and Korvin asked, "What is your government?" The Ruler's advisor explained their government as a system where the Ruler orders and all subjects obey, ensuring uniformity and swift action.

"You have heard our government," the Ruler said. "Now, define yours."  
Korvin nodded. "If you insist, I'll try. But you won't understand."  
The Ruler frowned. "We shall. Begin. Who governs you?"  
"None," Korvin said.  
"But you are governed?"  
"Yes," Korvin nodded. "But everyone is the governor."  
"Then there is no government," the Ruler said. "No single decision."  
"No," Korvin said. "Many decisions binding on all."  
"Who makes them binding?"  
"Many are unfavorable," Korvin said. "But we're not forced to accept them."  
"Do you act against your own interests?"  
"Not knowingly," Korvin shrugged. The lie-detector confirmed his truth. "I told you, you wouldn't understand."  
"It's a defect in your explanation," the Ruler snarled

Korvin calmly stated they had no need for a ruler, explaining their government was democracy, where people rule themselves. An expert dismissed this as impossible without force, but Korvin insisted. The expert accused Korvin of lying, and the technicians argued about a faulty machine. Korvin grew homesick and predicted a long wait. However, he escaped in three days when the experts were distracted, exploiting their inability to grasp mental sciences. His escape was made easier by their subconscious actions, leading to a quick departure from the planet.

He set his course, relaxed, and sent a message. Mission accomplished; the Tr'en would be occupied with indigestible thoughts, unable to digest them without becoming more democratic. They lacked the mental sciences for translation and insight. He wished he were home, bored stiff. THE END
