CAPTAIN CHAOS
By NELSON S. BOND
The Callisto-bound Leo needed a cook but got a jinxed Jonah instead. On Phobos, their regular cook fell ill, and they couldn't turn back. The skipper ordered Dugan to find a cook, but with the war and the scarcity of cooks, Dugan struggled. He tried restaurants, agencies, hotels, and even appealed to the wealthy colonists, but found no luck. Returning to the ship, Dugan suggested he could cook in a pinch.

"Thanks, Dugan, but that won't do. On this trip, the men must be fed regularly and well. Makeshift meals are O.K. on an ordinary run, but not when running the blockade. I caught his slip. 'The blockade, sir? Then you've read our orders?' The Old Man nodded. 'Yes. You might as well know, Lieutenant. We are to run the Outer Planets Alliance blockade and verify a loyalist uprising on Callisto. If true, we'll make a treaty. Our task is tough; we must find a cook to keep our men in top physical condition.'"

"The search is over. Where's the galley?" an oddly high-pitched voice asked impatiently. Facing us was a slim, young Earthman, barely five-foot-two, in a spaceman's uniform three sizes too large, holding a large ray-pistol and a carving knife. "Well, where is it?" he repeated. The Old Man stared, confused. "Who are you?" he asked. "I'm your new cook, Andy Laney," the stranger replied. "You don't look like much of a cook," the Old Man said. "And you don't look like much of a skipper," Andy retorted. The captain, flustered, agreed to hire him. "Get to the galley on the second level, port side. Mess in three quarters of an hour. Lift gravs immediately," he ordered.

"What are you doing at that table?" the little boy asked, peering at the trajectory charts. "We're going to Vesta, trying to run the Alliance blockade, right?" he piped. O'Hara roared, "None of your business! Get below!" The new chef suggested an alternative route, but the Old Man ordered him to leave. Hurt, the boy followed, apologizing. "You must learn not to speak out of turn," Dugan told him sternly. "The Old Man doesn't need advice from a cook." The boy argued he knew the Meteor Bog well, but Dugan dismissed him, telling him to focus on cooking.

I left, slamming the door. We headed to Vesta, and the Old Man announced our destination. The crew was excited, not scared. John Wainwright was eager, hoping to encounter the Alliance ships. Blinky Todd expressed satisfaction at the prospect of meeting them. A black-gang blaster showed his determination silently. The new Slops, busy preparing lunch, missed the announcement. His meal was delicious, even the Old Man admitted.

"Was everything all right, sir?" he asked.
"Not only all right, Slops," wheezed Captain O'Hara, "but perfect! Did you find everything O.Q. in the galley?"
"Captain Slops" blushed and fidgeted.
"Oh, thank you, sir! Yes, the galley was in fine order. But I need an incinerator. The current garbage-disposal system is old-fashioned and unsanitary. I have to carry waste down two levels to expel it."
The skipper frowned.
"I'm sorry, Slops, but we don't have the equipment. After this jump, I'll see what I can do."
"Oh, I've figured out a way to use an old Nolan heat-cannon as an incinerator," said Slops shyly.
"I said, hold everything, Slops! That's against regulations."
Our little chef's face fell. "I was planning

The skipper, Captain David O'Hara, eagerly agreed to move the old Nolan heat cannon to the galley for cooking, despite it being against the rules. Chef Slops, seemingly grateful, assured the captain he would start cooking with all the fixings once the cannon was installed. During the night, Slops was found zealously cleaning the cannon, nearly jumping in surprise when caught by the lieutenant.

"If you ask me, it looks lethal. The Old Man must be off his gravs letting you handle that toy. 'But I'm only going to use it to dispose of garbage,' he said. 'Don't dump cans when ships are around,' I warned. 'There'll be human scraps littering the void.' 'Yes, sir,' said Slops meekly. 'I'll be careful.' I sniggered, remembering a joke. 'Did you hear the one about the old maid at the Martian baths?' 'I've got to go now, Lieutenant,' shouted Slops, fleeing pink and flustered."

So there was one for the log-book! Our emergency chef lacked humor and was bashful. I shrugged and went back to the control turret. This happened on the first day out of Mars, a hundred million miles to the asteroid belt. The ship, the
Leo
, traveled at 400,000 m.p.h., taking at least ten days to reach Vesta. Captain Slops, a prude and kill-joy, refused to help with a prank, isolating himself from the crew.

He was always snooping and asking questions, earning him the nickname "Captain Slops." Despite his constant advice, the skipper remained in command. On the eleventh day, Slops warned about the danger of encountering enemy ships near Vesta due to recent ekalastron deposits and the orbit's aphelion stage. The skipper dismissed his concerns, leading to potential trouble.

"Don't give me lady-logic!" roared the Old Man, now furious. "If you'd told me the truth, I'd have listened. We're on a vital mission and now in danger. He rushed to the audio, ordering a new course through the Belt by way of Iris and the Bog. Suddenly, the ship shook violently, gripped by a tractor beam. Officers Wainwright and Bramble responded swiftly. Over the audio, voices called for Captain O'Hara, Chief McMurtrie asked about breaking the tractor beams, and the fore-gunnery port fired. An Alliance commander demanded surrender, surrounded by six armed craft. Wainwright urged resistance, but the Old Man, shaken, surrendered to avoid unnecessary sacrifice.

The firing stopped, and a deathly silence fell over the Leo. Andy Laney, frozen in the galley doorway, questioned their surrender. The Old Man glared silently. I snapped at Andy, telling him surrender was our only chance to survive and possibly escape later. Andy feared becoming prisoners and wondered about the Leo's fate. Realizing the danger of revealing their mission to Jupiter, I began to respect Andy's foresight. The skipper agreed, realizing the risk of exposing their secret to Callisto.

He would have ordered us to keep fighting, even to our deaths, but it was too late. The lock had already opened, and we heard the invaders' footsteps. The Alliance commandant smiled triumphantly as he entered.

What does the gold band that Ro put on Na's wrist mean for them?

 (A) They are engaged. 
 (B) They are combat mates.

references: They are married.

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
"You're home," breathed Na.
"I have traveled far to the north," answered Ro simply, "and seen many things. And now I have returned for you."
"They must have been great things you saw," Na coaxed.
"Yes, great and many. But that tale can wait. Tell me first how you came to be playing tag with the Oan."
Na lowered her eyes.
"I was caught in the forest below the cliffs. The Oan spied me and I ran. The chase was long and tiring. I was almost ready to drop when you appeared."
"You were alone in the woods!" Ro exclaimed. "Since when do the women of our tribe travel from the cliffs alone?"
"Since a long time," she answered sadly. Then she cried. And between sobs she spoke:
"Many weeks ago a great noise came out of the sky. We ran to the mouths of our caves and looked out, and saw a great sphere of shining metal landing in the valley below. Many colored fire spat from one end of it.
"The men of our tribe snatched up stones, and holding one in their hands and one beneath their armpits, they climbed down to battle or greet our visitors. They had surrounded the sphere and were waiting, when suddenly an entrance appeared in the metal and two men stepped out.
"They were strange men indeed; white as the foam on water, and clothed in strange garb from the neck down, even to coverings on their feet. They made signs of peace—with one hand only, for they carried weapons of a sort in the other. And the men of our tribe made the same one-handed sign of peace, for they would not risk dropping their stones. Then the white men spoke; but their tongue was strange, and our men signaled that they could not understand. The white men smiled, and a great miracle took place. Suddenly to our minds came pictures and words. The white men spoke with their thoughts.
"They came from a place called Earth, they said. And they came in peace. Our men found they could think very hard and answer back with their own thoughts. And there was much talk and happiness, for friendly visitors were always welcome.
"There were two more white ones who came from the sphere. One was a woman with golden hair, and the other, a man of age, with hair like silver frost.
"There was a great feast then, and our men showed their skill at throwing. Then the white men displayed the power of their strange weapons by pointing them at a tree and causing flame to leap forth to burn the wood in two. We were indeed glad they came in peace.

read_agent_gists:
COMING OF THE GODS
By CHESTER WHITEHORN

Ro, familiar with Mars' dangerous jungles, cautiously returned home after exploring strange northern villages. Weary of adventure, he longed to see his family and Na, his love. Reaching the jungle's edge, he smiled, quickening his pace on the safer plain. Suddenly, he halted, spotting a figure pursued by another.

Ro studied the approaching figures, gasping when he recognized the pursued woman. The pursuer was a squat, ugly Oan rat man. Ro found a rock, ran forward, and threw it at the Oan, who was too focused on his prey to notice. The stone hit the Oan, killing him instantly. Ro then embraced the woman, who was overjoyed to see him.

"You're home," breathed Na.
"I've returned from the north, having seen many things," answered Ro. "Tell me first about playing tag with the Oan."
Na looked down. "I was caught in the forest and ran from the Oan. The chase was long and tiring. You appeared just in time."
"You were alone?" Ro exclaimed. "Women don't travel alone from the cliffs."
"Since a long time," she answered sadly, then cried. "Many weeks ago, a metal sphere landed in the valley with a loud noise. Our men went to greet it, carrying stones. Two white men stepped out, holding weapons. They communicated telepathically, saying they came from Earth in peace. Two more arrived: a golden-haired woman and a silver-haired man. They showed us their weapons, and we celebrated."

That night, they asked their friends to join them in the caves, but the friends camped in the valley. With dawn, they found their friends captured and killed by the Oan using weapons. A three-day battle ensued, and they fled under cover of night. Ro, upon hearing this, vowed revenge, determined to free the captured white men and use their weapons against the Oan. Na, concerned, warned of the Oan's numbers, but Ro remained resolute. As the sun set, they reached the cliffs, spotting the metal sphere and the Oan below. Ro planned to rescue the white men and learn more about them. He left Na, giving her a gold arm band as a symbol of their bond, and disappeared over the cliff.

Ro carried weighted stones in his left hand and under his armpit for defense, with his right arm free for climbing. He climbed a smooth, dark wall, reaching crevices with his fingers and finding footholds with his toes. The night was perilously dark, and he rested only briefly before moving cautiously toward the camp, avoiding nearby Oan. He took a stone from his armpit and halted when an Oan guard approached. Remembering his mother's warning about the Oan's keen eyesight, Ro attacked silently, cracking the guard's skull and disposing of the body noiselessly.

Breathing easier, Ro moved silently to the edge of the clearing, where slumbering rat men lay. He inched forward on his stomach, scratched and painless, his heart singing. Spotting a golden mass, he freed a girl who communicated telepathically, urging him to hurry. Freeing her, he warned her to stay still and promised to signal her when to follow. As he freed others, his confidence grew.

When ready, Ro led the four white people into the woods. The girl whispered, and Ro glared. They moved slowly at first but sped up as silence became less crucial. Ro explained their route and revealed Na as his chosen mate. The man with silver hair questioned Ro's rescue motives, to which Ro replied he aimed to avenge his family and tribe. They waited for Na but encountered a rat man carrying a girl instead. Ro attacked, overpowering the rat man in a fierce struggle, ultimately killing him.

Ro dismounted the limp body, his face shifting from triumph to concern as he remembered the girl. He rushed to her side as she opened her eyes, fearfully looking around before smiling at him. Na recounted her mistake, thinking the rat man was Ro. After comforting her, they traveled to a hidden cave, where Ro assured them they'd stay until ready to attack the Oan. Despite the six against hundreds, Ro remained confident, planning to discuss further at dawn. He observed the group, noting the wise old man, the handsome but pale men, and the beautiful girl with golden hair.

"Tell me, where is your home and how do you speak with minds?" the old man answered for the group from Earth, explaining their expedition to Mars. Ro and Na introduced themselves, curious about Earth's technology and time measurement. The Earth visitors described their advanced telepathy skills, which Ro found fascinating but admitted Mars was behind in such knowledge. They decided to rest for the next busy day.

Ro slipped his arm around Na's shoulder as they slept. Upon waking, he found her gone and scolded her for wandering, though he helped her with the fruits and berries she had gathered. Noticing the awkwardness between Charlotte and Carlson, Ro realized the white men were no different from Martians in their behavior. After breakfast, when asked about attacking the Oan, Ro grew angry at Grimm's claim that they couldn't make weapons. Ro fell silent, then proposed a plan to the group. He suggested Na could lure the Oan guards away by revealing herself, allowing others to take the unguarded sphere. Despite concerns about the risk, Ro assured them he would be nearby, using rocks to fend off the Oan while Na led them through a narrow valley. Grimm reluctantly agreed to go with Ro, while Carlson, Charlotte, and the professor would take the sphere. After detailing the plan, they left the cave and headed towards the Oan camp.

Ro was proud of Na's fearlessness. Carlson watched Charlotte with a similar expression. Ro urged them to keep moving to avoid Oan strategies. They climbed in silence, and Ro paused to look back, feeling regret as Na and the others disappeared. At the top, Ro and Carlson prepared a rockfall trap over the valley's narrowest part. Ro teased Carlson about his feelings for Charlotte, suggesting fear of Grimm was the reason for not saying goodbye.

Carlson straightened, pondering Ro's words. "I didn't want trouble. Charlotte and I plan to marry in America. Grimm isn't a factor. He'll understand."

"Why not marry her here on Mars?"

Carlson looked surprised. "It's not legal. Who would officiate?"

Ro laughed. "On Mars, we take our mates. No one questions legality. A kiss and a promise are enough."

Carlson chuckled. "I could argue, but Hollywood's marriage chaos makes your system seem simpler. Here, a kiss and promise are binding."

Ro remained silent, his thoughts unshared, focusing on the pressing matter at hand.

"Yes, great and many. But that tale can wait. Tell me first how you came to be playing tag with the Oan."
Na lowered her eyes.
"I was caught in the forest below the cliffs. The Oan spied me and I ran. The chase was long and tiring. I was almost ready to drop when you appeared."
"You were alone in the woods!" Ro exclaimed. "Since when do the women of our tribe travel from the cliffs alone?"

And the men of our tribe made the same one-handed sign of peace, for they would not risk dropping their stones. Then the white men spoke; but their tongue was strange, and our men signaled that they could not understand. The white men smiled, and a great miracle took place. Suddenly to our minds came pictures and words. The white men spoke with their thoughts.
"They came from a place called Earth, they said. And they came in peace. Our men found they could think very hard and answer back with their own thoughts. And there was much talk and happiness, for friendly visitors were always welcome.
"There were two more white ones who came from the sphere. One was a woman with golden hair, and the other, a man of age, with hair like silver frost.

Suddenly he halted. A dim figure approached. It was one of the Oan, a guard. He was coming straight at Ro. The young Martian shrank back.
"The rat men have eyes to cut the night." It was a memory of his mother's voice. She had spoken those words when he was a child, to keep him from straying too far.
The Oan was only a few feet away now, but his eyes were not cutting the night. Ro could see his large ears, hear his twitching tail. In a moment the beast would stumble over him.
Like a phantom, Ro arose from his crouch. The rat man was startled, frozen with fear. Ro drove his right arm around. The stone in his hand cracked the Oan's skull like an eggshell. Ro caught the body as it fell, lowered it noiselessly to the ground.

read_agent_gists:
COMING OF THE GODS
By CHESTER WHITEHORN

Ro, familiar with Mars' dangerous jungles, cautiously returned home after exploring strange northern villages. Weary of adventure, he longed to see his family and Na, his love. Reaching the plain, he smiled, but halted when spotting a figure being chased.

Ro studied the approaching figures, gasping when he recognized the pursued woman. The pursuer was a squat, ugly Oan rat man. Ro's face turned grim as he picked up a rock and ran towards them. The Oan, focused on his prey, didn't notice Ro until it was too late. Ro threw the rock, hitting the Oan in the side and killing him. The woman, glad to be saved, embraced Ro, who couldn't help but smile despite trying to appear stern.

"You're home," breathed Na.
"I've returned from the north, having seen many things," answered Ro. "Tell me first about playing tag with the Oan."
Na looked down. "I was caught in the forest and ran from the Oan. The chase was long and tiring. You appeared just in time."
"You were alone?" Ro exclaimed. "Women don't travel alone from the cliffs."
"Since a long time," she answered sadly, then cried. "Many weeks ago, a metal sphere landed in the valley with a loud noise. Our men went to greet it, carrying stones. Two white men stepped out, holding weapons. They communicated telepathically, saying they came from Earth in peace. Two more arrived: a golden-haired woman and a silver-haired man. They showed us their weapons, and we celebrated."

That night, they asked their friends to join them in the caves, but the friends camped in the valley. With dawn, they found their friends captured and killed by the Oan using weapons. A three-day battle ensued, and they fled under cover of night. Ro, upon hearing this, vowed revenge, determined to free the captured white men and use their weapons against the Oan. Na, concerned, warned of the Oan's numbers. Ro, undeterred, planned to rescue the white men and learn more about them. As night fell, he bid farewell to Na, marking her as his mate before leaving to face the Oan.

Ro carried stones in his left hand and under his armpit, ready to attack any Oan who crossed his path. His right arm was free for climbing, finding crevices and footholds in the dark. The long, dangerous climb left him sweating, but he reached the bottom and moved cautiously toward the camp, avoiding detection. Ahead, he saw a clearing where he would find the white ones. Suddenly, a guard approached. Remembering his mother's warning about the Oan's keen eyesight, Ro struck the guard silently, ensuring no alarm was raised.

Breathing easier, Ro silently reached the clearing's edge. On the ground, slumbering rat men lay. He lay flat, inching forward cautiously. Scratched but painless, he felt victory near. Spotting a golden mass, he freed a girl who communicated telepathically. Urging her to stay still, he promised to signal when ready. Freeing her, he moved to untie others, growing more confident.

When ready, Ro led the four white people into the woods. The girl whispered, and Ro glared. They moved slowly at first but sped up as silence became less crucial. Ro explained their route and revealed Na as his chosen mate. The man with silver hair questioned Ro's rescue motives, to which Ro replied he aimed to avenge his family and tribe. They waited for Na but encountered a rat man carrying a girl instead. Ro attacked, overpowering the rat man in a fierce struggle, ultimately killing him.

Ro dismounted the limp body, his face shifting from triumph to concern as he remembered the girl. He rushed to her side as she opened her eyes, fearfully looking around before smiling at him. Na recounted her mistake, thinking the rat man was Ro. After comforting her, they traveled to a hidden cave, where Ro assured them they'd stay until ready to attack the Oan. Despite the six against hundreds, Ro remained confident, planning to discuss further at dawn. He observed the group, noting the wise old man, the handsome but pale men, and the beautiful girl with golden hair.

"Tell me, where is this strange place you come from, and how can you speak with minds?" the old man asked.

"We're from Earth, millions of miles away. We're an expedition to study Mars," the lean man, Carlson, and the stouter man, Grimm, introduced themselves along with their leader.

"What's Earth like?" Ro asked.

"Our part, America, has steel and stone cities, and we travel in space boats. What year is it here?" the old man inquired.

"You've seen it," Ro replied. "We don't measure time by years."

"A year is a time measure. It was 2200 when we left Earth," the old man explained.

"We don't have that here," Ro said. "But how do you speak with minds?"

"It's telepathy. We learn it from childhood. It's like sending pictures in a universal language," the old man said.

Ro sighed

Ro slipped his arm around Na's shoulder as they slept. Upon waking, he found her gone and scolded her for wandering, though he helped her with the fruits and berries she had gathered. Noticing Charlotte's shyness and Carlson's annoyance, Ro realized the men were no different from Martians in their treatment of women. After breakfast, Carlson questioned their plan to attack the Oan. Ro, angered by Grimm's claim that they lacked the resources to make weapons, was calmed by the professor's suggestion to use weapons from their ship, though they faced the challenge of reaching it due to its heavy guard.

Ro fell silent, then proposed a plan to the group. He suggested Na could lure the Oan guards away by revealing herself, allowing others to take the unguarded sphere. The professor objected, but Ro assured he would protect Na from above while Grimm and Carlson would assist. After a brief altercation, they agreed on roles and left the cave to head towards the Oan camp.

They moved swiftly to the narrow valley entrance, about a hundred yards long and twenty feet wide, with steep cliffs on both sides. Ro left the professor with Na, who would guide him to the sphere. Ro was proud of Na's fearlessness. Carlson watched Charlotte with a similar expression. Ro urged them to keep moving to avoid Oan strategies. They climbed in silence, and Ro paused to look back, feeling regret as Na and the others disappeared. At the top, Ro and Carlson prepared a rockfall trap over the valley's narrowest part. Ro teased Carlson about his feelings for Charlotte, suggesting fear of Grimm was the reason for not saying goodbye.

Carlson, after considering Ro's words, admitted he and Charlotte planned to marry in America, not seeing Grimm as a threat. Ro suggested marrying on Mars to end the conflict, pointing out that legality and ceremonies were irrelevant on Mars. Ro believed a kiss and a promise were sufficient. Carlson agreed, noting that while it might seem chaotic by Earth standards, it was likely binding on Mars. Ro remained silent, finding the situation more pressing than unraveling the complexities of Earth's marriage customs.

query:
Why is Grimm annoyed that Charlotte slept beside Carlson?

 (A) Because he is Charlotte's friend and he doesn't think that Carlson is good enough for her. 
 (B) Because he is Charlotte's father and does not approve of the relationship. 
 (C) Because he is the leader of the expedition and doesn't want his crew to get distracted with romance. 
 (D) Because he is in love with Charlotte and is jealous of the affection between her and Carlson.
references: Because he is in love with Charlotte and is jealous of the affection between her and Carlson.

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
Ro slipped his arm about Na's shoulder and drew her closer. With their heads together they slept.
Ro awakened with the dawn. He was startled to find that Na had left his side. He rose quickly and strode to the mouth of the cave.
Na met him at the entrance. She was returning from a clump of trees a short distance away. Her arms were loaded with Manno, the fruit of Mars, and clusters of wild berries and grapes.
"You see," she said, "I will make you a good mate. Our table will be well provided for."
"You will make no mate at all," Ro said sternly, "and there will be no table if you wander off. Your next meeting with the Oan may not be so fortunate."
He glared at her for a moment, then smiled and helped her with her burden.
The others in the cave awakened. Ro noticed that Charlotte had slept beside Carlson, but moved away shyly now that it was daylight. He noticed, too, that Grimm was seeing the same thing and seemed annoyed.
Ro smiled. These young white men were no different than Martians where a girl was concerned.
When they had finished breakfast, they sat around the floor of the cave and spoke.
It was Carlson who asked, "How do you expect the six of us to attack the rat men?"
"The Oan are cowards," Ro answered. "They are brave only because they have your weapons. But now that you are free, you can make more of these sticks that shoot fire."
Grimm laughed.
"It takes intricate machinery to construct a ray gun," he said. "Here in this wilderness we have sticks and stones to work with."
Ro sprang to his feet to tower above the man. His handsome face was twisted in anger.
"You're lying," he shouted aloud, forgetting that the white man couldn't understand his words. "You're lying because you are afraid. You refuse to help me avenge my people because you are more of a coward than the Oan."
Grimm climbed to his feet and backed away. Ro advanced on him, his fists clenched.
The old man also rose. He placed a restraining hand on Ro's arm.
"He's lying," said Ro with his thoughts.
"Tell him I'm speaking the truth, professor," said Grimm aloud.
The professor repeated Grimm's words with his thoughts. "It would be impossible to make new guns here," he said. "But there is another way. I have thought about it all night."
Ro turned quickly.
"What is it?" he demanded.
"The space sphere. There are weapons on our ship that are greater than ray guns. With those we could defeat the rat men." The professor shrugged, turned away. "But how could we get into the ship? It is too well guarded."

"You will make no mate at all," Ro said sternly, "and there will be no table if you wander off. Your next meeting with the Oan may not be so fortunate."
He glared at her for a moment, then smiled and helped her with her burden.
The others in the cave awakened. Ro noticed that Charlotte had slept beside Carlson, but moved away shyly now that it was daylight. He noticed, too, that Grimm was seeing the same thing and seemed annoyed.
Ro smiled. These young white men were no different than Martians where a girl was concerned.
When they had finished breakfast, they sat around the floor of the cave and spoke.
It was Carlson who asked, "How do you expect the six of us to attack the rat men?"
"The Oan are cowards," Ro answered. "They are brave only because they have your weapons. But now that you are free, you can make more of these sticks that shoot fire."
Grimm laughed.
"It takes intricate machinery to construct a ray gun," he said. "Here in this wilderness we have sticks and stones to work with."
Ro sprang to his feet to tower above the man. His handsome face was twisted in anger.
"You're lying," he shouted aloud, forgetting that the white man couldn't understand his words. "You're lying because you are afraid. You refuse to help me avenge my people because you are more of a coward than the Oan."
Grimm climbed to his feet and backed away. Ro advanced on him, his fists clenched.
The old man also rose. He placed a restraining hand on Ro's arm.
"He's lying," said Ro with his thoughts.
"Tell him I'm speaking the truth, professor," said Grimm aloud.
The professor repeated Grimm's words with his thoughts. "It would be impossible to make new guns here," he said. "But there is another way. I have thought about it all night."
Ro turned quickly.
"What is it?" he demanded.
"The space sphere. There are weapons on our ship that are greater than ray guns. With those we could defeat the rat men." The professor shrugged, turned away. "But how could we get into the ship? It is too well guarded."
Ro fell silent, then proposed a plan to the group. He suggested Na could lure the Oan guards away by revealing herself, allowing others to take the unguarded sphere. The professor objected, but Ro assured he would protect Na from above while Grimm and Carlson would assist. After a brief altercation, they agreed on roles and left the cave to head towards the Oan camp.
They moved swiftly to the narrow valley entrance, about a hundred yards long and twenty feet wide, with steep cliffs on both sides. Ro left the professor with Na, who would guide him to the sphere. Ro was proud of Na's fearlessness. Carlson watched Charlotte with a similar expression. Ro urged them to keep moving to avoid Oan strategies. They climbed in silence, and Ro paused to look back, feeling regret as Na and the others disappeared. At the top, Ro and Carlson prepared a rockfall trap over the valley's narrowest part. Ro teased Carlson about his feelings for Charlotte, suggesting fear of Grimm was the reason for not saying goodbye.
Carlson, after considering Ro's words, admitted he and Charlotte planned to marry in America, not seeing Grimm as a threat. Ro suggested marrying on Mars to end the conflict, pointing out that legality and ceremonies were irrelevant on Mars. Ro believed a kiss and a promise were sufficient. Carlson agreed, noting that while it might seem chaotic by Earth standards, it was likely binding on Mars. Ro remained silent, finding the situation more pressing than unraveling the complexities of Earth's marriage customs.

Why is Grimm annoyed that Charlotte slept beside Carlson?

 (A) Because he is Charlotte's friend and he doesn't think that Carlson is good enough for her. 
 (B) Because he is Charlotte's father and does not approve of the relationship. 
 (C) Because he is the leader of the expedition and doesn't want his crew to get distracted with romance. 
 (D) Because he is in love with Charlotte and is jealous of the affection between her and Carlson.

Answer with ONLY the exact text of the correct option, without the option letter, parentheses, or any extra words.
prediction: Because he is in love with Charlotte and is jealous of the affection between her and Carlson.

In what sense does Ro relate to the white young men?

 (A) In their difficulty understanding signals that women send them. 
 (B) In their eagerness to enter into combat situations. 
 (C) In their need to establish themselves as the more dominant male through physical prowess. 
 (D) In their attachment to and rivalry over women.

Ro fell silent. He walked to the mouth of the cave and stared out. When he turned back to the others, his attention was centered on Na.
"Perhaps the attraction you seem to hold for the Oan can be put to good use," he said aloud. "The sphere is a distance away from the Oan camp. All of the rat men cannot be guarding it. Perhaps, by revealing yourself, you can lure the guards away from their post."
He repeated his plan to the others.
"But they'll kill her," gasped Charlotte.
"She will be a woman alone," said Ro. "The Oan prefer to capture women when they can."
"Then she'll be captured," the professor said. "It's much too risky."
Ro laughed.
"Do you think I will let her go alone? I will be close by. Na can lead the rat men through a narrow part of the valley. I will be above on the cliffs, waiting to pelt them with stones. Carlson or Grimm can be with me to roll an avalanche of rocks on their heads.
"In the meantime, you can take over the unguarded sphere. The rest will be easy."
The professor smacked his fist into his palm.
"It might work at that. Grimm can go with you. Carlson and Charlotte will go with me."
"Why me?" Grimm demanded. "Why not Carlson? Or are you saving him for your daughter?"
Carlson grabbed Grimm by the shoulder and spun him around. He drove a hard fist into the stout man's face.
Grimm stumbled backward. He fell at the cave's entrance. His hand, sprawled behind him to stop his fall, closed over a rock. He flung it at Carlson from a sitting position. It caught Carlson in the shoulder.
Gritting his teeth, Carlson charged at Grimm. But Ro moved more swiftly. He caught the white man and forced him back.
"This is no time for fighting," he said. "When the Oan are defeated you can kill each other. But not until then."
Grimm brushed himself off as he got to his feet
"Okay," he sneered. "I'll go with the red man. But when we meet again, it will be a different story."
Carlson turned to Ro.
"I'll go with you," he said. "Grimm can go with Charlotte and the professor."
When they had detailed their plan, the party left the cave. Ro led them into the thickest part of the forest and toward the Oan camp.

read_agent_gists:
COMING OF THE GODS
By CHESTER WHITEHORN

Ro, familiar with Mars' dangerous jungles, cautiously returned home after exploring strange northern villages. Weary of adventure, he longed to see his family and Na, his love. Reaching the plain, he smiled, but halted when spotting a figure being chased.

Ro studied the approaching figures, gasping when he recognized the pursued woman. The pursuer was a squat, ugly Oan rat man. Ro's face turned grim as he picked up a rock and ran towards them. The Oan, focused on his prey, didn't notice Ro until it was too late. Ro threw the rock, hitting the Oan in the side and killing him. The woman, glad to be saved, embraced Ro, who couldn't help but smile despite trying to appear stern.

"You're home," breathed Na.
"I've returned from the north, having seen many things," answered Ro. "Tell me first about playing tag with the Oan."
Na looked down. "I was caught in the forest and ran from the Oan. The chase was long and tiring. You appeared just in time."
"You were alone?" Ro exclaimed. "Women don't travel alone from the cliffs."
"Since a long time," she answered sadly, then cried. "Many weeks ago, a metal sphere landed in the valley with a loud noise. Our men went to greet it, carrying stones. Two white men stepped out, holding weapons. They communicated telepathically, saying they came from Earth in peace. Two more arrived: a golden-haired woman and a silver-haired man. They showed us their weapons, and we celebrated."

That night, they asked their friends to join them in the caves, but the friends camped in the valley. With dawn, they found their friends captured and killed by the Oan using weapons. A three-day battle ensued, and they fled under cover of night. Ro, upon hearing this, vowed revenge, determined to free the captured white men and use their weapons against the Oan. Na, concerned, warned of the Oan's numbers. Ro, undeterred, planned to rescue the white men and learn more about them. As night fell, he bid farewell to Na, marking her as his mate before leaving to face the Oan.

Ro carried stones in his left hand and under his armpit, ready to attack any Oan who crossed his path. His right arm was free for climbing, finding crevices and footholds in the dark. The long, dangerous climb left him sweating, but he reached the bottom and moved cautiously toward the camp, avoiding detection. Ahead, he saw a clearing where he would find the white ones. Suddenly, a guard approached. Remembering his mother's warning about the Oan's keen eyesight, Ro struck the guard silently, ensuring no alarm was raised.

Breathing easier, Ro silently reached the clearing's edge. On the ground, slumbering rat men lay. He lay flat, inching forward cautiously. Scratched but painless, he felt victory near. Spotting a golden mass, he freed a girl who communicated telepathically. Urging her to stay still, he promised to signal when ready. Freeing her, he moved to untie others, growing more confident.

When ready, Ro led the four white people into the woods. The girl whispered, and Ro glared. They moved slowly at first but sped up as silence became less crucial. Ro explained their route and revealed Na as his chosen mate. The man with silver hair questioned Ro's rescue motives, to which Ro replied he aimed to avenge his family and tribe. They waited for Na but encountered a rat man carrying a girl instead. Ro attacked, overpowering the rat man in a fierce struggle, ultimately killing him.

Ro dismounted the limp body, his face shifting from triumph to concern as he remembered the girl. He rushed to her side as she opened her eyes, fearfully looking around before smiling at him. Na recounted her mistake, thinking the rat man was Ro. After comforting her, they traveled to a hidden cave, where Ro assured them they'd stay until ready to attack the Oan. Despite the six against hundreds, Ro remained confident, planning to discuss further at dawn. He observed the group, noting the wise old man, the handsome but pale men, and the beautiful girl with golden hair.

"Tell me, where is this strange place you come from, and how can you speak with minds?" the old man asked.

"We're from Earth, millions of miles away. We're an expedition to study Mars," the lean man, Carlson, and the stouter man, Grimm, introduced themselves along with their leader.

"What's Earth like?" Ro asked.

"Our part, America, has steel and stone cities, and we travel in space boats. What year is it here?" the old man inquired.

"You've seen it," Ro replied. "We don't measure time by years."

"A year is a time measure. It was 2200 when we left Earth," the old man explained.

"We don't have that here," Ro said. "But how do you speak with minds?"

"It's telepathy. We learn it from childhood. It's like sending pictures in a universal language," the old man said.

Ro sighed

Ro slipped his arm around Na's shoulder as they slept. Upon waking, he found her gone and scolded her for wandering, though he helped her with the fruits and berries she had gathered. Noticing Charlotte's shyness and Carlson's annoyance, Ro realized the men were no different from Martians in their treatment of women. After breakfast, Carlson questioned their plan to attack the Oan. Ro, angered by Grimm's claim that they lacked the resources to make weapons, was calmed by the professor's suggestion to use weapons from their ship, though they faced the challenge of reaching it due to its heavy guard.

Ro fell silent, then proposed a plan to the group. He suggested Na could lure the Oan guards away by revealing herself, allowing others to take the unguarded sphere. The professor objected, but Ro assured he would protect Na from above while Grimm and Carlson would assist. After a brief altercation, they agreed on roles and left the cave to head towards the Oan camp.

They moved swiftly to the narrow valley entrance, about a hundred yards long and twenty feet wide, with steep cliffs on both sides. Ro left the professor with Na, who would guide him to the sphere. Ro was proud of Na's fearlessness. Carlson watched Charlotte with a similar expression. Ro urged them to keep moving to avoid Oan strategies. They climbed in silence, and Ro paused to look back, feeling regret as Na and the others disappeared. At the top, Ro and Carlson prepared a rockfall trap over the valley's narrowest part. Ro teased Carlson about his feelings for Charlotte, suggesting fear of Grimm was the reason for not saying goodbye.

Carlson, after considering Ro's words, admitted he and Charlotte planned to marry in America, not seeing Grimm as a threat. Ro suggested marrying on Mars to end the conflict, pointing out that legality and ceremonies were irrelevant on Mars. Ro believed a kiss and a promise were sufficient. Carlson agreed, noting that while it might seem chaotic by Earth standards, it was likely binding on Mars. Ro remained silent, finding the situation more pressing than unraveling the complexities of Earth's marriage customs.

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
"Tell me," he asked suddenly, "where is this strange place you come from? And how is it that you can speak and cause others to speak with their minds?"
It was the old man who answered.
"We come from a place called Earth, many millions of miles away through space. My daughter, Charlotte, my two assistants, Carlson—" the lean man nodded—"Grimm—" the stouter man acknowledged the introduction—"and myself are an expedition. We came here to Mars to study."
Ro introduced himself and Na.
"What manner of a place is this Earth?" he asked, after the formalities.
"Our part of Earth, America, is a great country. Our cities are built of steel and stone, and we travel about in space boats. Now tell me, what is it like here on Mars? Surely the whole planet isn't wilderness. What year is it?"
"You have seen what it is like here," Ro answered. "As for 'year,' I don't understand."
"A year is a measure of time," the old man explained. "When we left Earth it was the year twenty-two hundred."
"We have nothing like that here," said Ro, still puzzled. "But tell me, about this speaking with the mind. Perhaps I shall understand that."
"It's simple telepathy. We have mastered the science on Earth. It takes study from childhood, but once you have mastered the art, it is quite simple to transmit or receive thoughts from anyone. A mere matter of concentration. We—who speak different tongues—understand each other because of action we have in mind as we speak. We want the other to walk, we think of the other walking. A picture is transmitted and understood. It is a message in a Universal language."
Ro sighed.
"I am afraid we are very backward here on Mars," he said wearily. "I would like to learn more, but we must sleep now. Tomorrow will be a very busy day."

After breakfast, Carlson questioned their plan to attack the Oan. Ro, angered by Grimm's claim that they lacked the resources to make weapons, was calmed by the professor's suggestion to use weapons from their ship, though they faced the challenge of reaching it due to its heavy guard.

Ro fell silent, then proposed a plan to the group. He suggested Na could lure the Oan guards away by revealing herself, allowing others to take the unguarded sphere. The professor objected, but Ro assured he would protect Na from above while Grimm and Carlson would assist. After a brief altercation, they agreed on roles and left the cave to head towards the Oan camp.

They moved swiftly to the narrow valley entrance, about a hundred yards long and twenty feet wide, with steep cliffs on both sides. Ro left the professor with Na, who would guide him to the sphere. Ro was proud of Na's fearlessness. Carlson watched Charlotte with a similar expression. Ro urged them to keep moving to avoid Oan strategies. They climbed in silence, and Ro paused to look back, feeling regret as Na and the others disappeared. At the top, Ro and Carlson prepared a rockfall trap over the valley's narrowest part. Ro teased Carlson about his feelings for Charlotte, suggesting fear of Grimm was the reason for not saying goodbye.

Carlson, after considering Ro's words, admitted he and Charlotte planned to marry in America, not seeing Grimm as a threat. Ro suggested marrying on Mars to end the conflict, pointing out that legality and ceremonies were irrelevant on Mars. Ro believed a kiss and a promise were sufficient. Carlson agreed, noting that while it might seem chaotic by Earth standards, it was likely binding on Mars. The Oan, focused on his prey, didn't notice Ro until it was too late. Ro threw the rock, hitting the Oan in the side and killing him. The woman, glad to be saved, embraced Ro, who couldn't help but smile despite trying to appear stern.
"You're home," breathed Na.
"I've returned from the north, having seen many things," answered Ro. "Tell me first about playing tag with the Oan."
Na looked down. "I was caught in the forest and ran from the Oan. The chase was long and tiring. You appeared just in time."
"You were alone?" Ro exclaimed. "Women don't travel alone from the cliffs."
"Since a long time," she answered sadly, then cried. "Many weeks ago, a metal sphere landed in the valley with a loud noise. Our men went to greet it, carrying stones. Two white men stepped out, holding weapons. They communicated telepathically, saying they came from Earth in peace. Two more arrived: a golden-haired woman and a silver-haired man. They showed us their weapons, and we celebrated."
That night, they asked their friends to join them in the caves, but the friends camped in the valley. With dawn, they found their friends captured and killed by the Oan using weapons. A three-day battle ensued, and they fled under cover of night. Ro, upon hearing this, vowed revenge, determined to free the captured white men and use their weapons against the Oan. Na, concerned, warned of the Oan's numbers. Ro, undeterred, planned to rescue the white men and learn more about them. As night fell, he bid farewell to Na, marking her as his mate before leaving to face the Oan.
Ro carried stones in his left hand and under his armpit, ready to attack any Oan who crossed his path. His right arm was free for climbing, finding crevices and footholds in the dark. The long, dangerous climb left him sweating, but he reached the bottom and moved cautiously toward the camp, avoiding detection. Ahead, he saw a clearing where he would find the white ones. Suddenly, a guard approached. Remembering his mother's warning about the Oan's keen eyesight, Ro struck the guard silently, ensuring no alarm was raised.
Breathing easier, Ro silently reached the clearing's edge. On the ground, slumbering rat men lay. He lay flat, inching forward cautiously. Scratched but painless, he felt victory near. Spotting a golden mass, he freed a girl who communicated telepathically. Urging her to stay still, he promised to signal when ready. Freeing her, he moved to untie others, growing more confident.
When ready, Ro led the four white people into the woods. The girl whispered, and Ro glared. They moved slowly at first but sped up as silence became less crucial. Ro explained their route and revealed Na as his chosen mate. The man with silver hair questioned Ro's rescue motives, to which Ro replied he aimed to avenge his family and tribe. They waited for Na but encountered a rat man carrying a girl instead. Ro attacked, overpowering the rat man in a fierce struggle, ultimately killing him.
Ro dismounted the limp body, his face shifting from triumph to concern as he remembered the girl. He rushed to her side as she opened her eyes, fearfully looking around before smiling at him. Na recounted her mistake, thinking the rat man was Ro. After comforting her, they traveled to a hidden cave, where Ro assured them they'd stay until ready to attack the Oan. Despite the six against hundreds, Ro remained confident, planning to discuss further at dawn. He observed the group, noting the wise old man, the handsome but pale men, and the beautiful girl with golden hair.
"Tell me," he asked suddenly, "where is this strange place you come from? And how is it that you can speak and cause others to speak with their minds?"
It was the old man who answered.
"We come from a place called Earth, many millions of miles away through space. My daughter, Charlotte, my two assistants, Carlson—" the lean man nodded—"Grimm—" the stouter man acknowledged the introduction—"and myself are an expedition. We came here to Mars to study."
Ro introduced himself and Na.
"What manner of a place is this Earth?" he asked, after the formalities.
"Our part of Earth, America, is a great country. Our cities are built of steel and stone, and we travel about in space boats. Now tell me, what is it like here on Mars? Surely the whole planet isn't wilderness. What year is it?"
"You have seen what it is like here," Ro answered. "As for 'year,' I don't understand."
"A year is a measure of time," the old man explained. "When we left Earth it was the year twenty-two hundred."
"We have nothing like that here," said Ro, still puzzled. "But tell me, about this speaking with the mind. Perhaps I shall understand that."
"It's simple telepathy. We have mastered the science on Earth. It takes study from childhood, but once you have mastered the art, it is quite simple to transmit or receive thoughts from anyone. A mere matter of concentration. We—who speak different tongues—understand each other because of action we have in mind as we speak. We want the other to walk, we think of the other walking. A picture is transmitted and understood. It is a message in a Universal language."
Ro sighed.
"I am afraid we are very backward here on Mars," he said wearily. "I would like to learn more, but we must sleep now. Tomorrow will be a very busy day."

Noticing Charlotte's shyness and Carlson's annoyance, Ro realized the men were no different from Martians in their treatment of women. Ro was proud of Na's fearlessness. Carlson watched Charlotte with a similar expression. Ro urged them to keep moving to avoid Oan strategies.  (A) their typical mode of communication
 (B) the importance of tracking time

query:
Why did Ro change his mind about the people on Mars being backwards?

 (A) Because he realized that despite human's technological advancements, they have over-complicated marriage. 
 (B) Because he realized that while the humans are physically vulnerable without their weapons, the red people have formidable strength in their arms. 
 (C) Because he realized that human males suppress public affection when they are intimidated by other males, whereas male Martians don't hide their affection. 
 (D) Because he realized that male humans were petty and even brute when it came to rivalry over women, whereas male Martians were much more civilized.
references: Because he realized that despite human's technological advancements, they have over-complicated marriage.

Carlson straightened. He weighed Ro's words before answering. Finally he said, "I didn't want to make trouble. It was a bad time, and senseless, besides. Charlotte and I are planning to be married when we return to America. It's not as though Grimm was still in the running. I'm sure he'll see reason when we tell him. It's foolish to be enemies."
"Why don't you take her for your wife here on Mars? That would end the trouble completely."
Carlson seemed surprised.
"It wouldn't be legal. Who would perform the ceremony?"
Ro seemed puzzled, then he laughed.
"Last night I thought that we on Mars are backward. Now I'm not so sure. When we find our mates here, we take her. There is no one to speak of 'legal' or 'ceremony.' After all, it's a personal matter. Who can tell us whether it is 'legal' or not? What better ceremony than a kiss and a promise?" He bent back to his work chuckling.
"I could argue the point," Carlson laughed. "I could tell you about a place called Hollywood. Marriage and divorce is bad enough there. Under your system, it would really be a mess. But I won't say anything. Here on Mars your kiss and a promise is probably as binding as any ceremony."
Ro didn't speak. He didn't concentrate and transmit his thoughts, but kept them to himself. The pictures he'd received from Carlson were confusing. The business at hand was more grim and important than untangling the puzzle.

Then he gave a glad cry. Squinting ahead he saw an approaching figure. It was—His cry took on a note of alarm. The figure was bent low under the weight of a burden. It was a rat man, and slung across his shoulders was a girl.
Ro's body tensed and quivered. A low growl issued from deep in his throat. He charged forward.
The Oan saw him coming and straightened, allowing the girl to fall. He set his twisted legs and bared his fangs. The fur on his back stood out straight as he prepared to meet the young Martian's attack.
Ro struck his foe head on. They went down in a frenzied bundle of fury. The rat man's tail lashed out to twist around Ro's neck. With frantic strength, Ro tore it away before it could tighten.
Ignoring the Oan's slashing teeth, the young Martian pounded heavy fists into his soft stomach. Suddenly shifting his attack, Ro wrapped his legs around the rat man's waist. His hands caught a furry throat and tightened.
Over and over they rolled. The Oan clawed urgently at the Martian's choking fingers. His chest made strange noises as it pleaded for the air that would give it life. But Ro's hands were bands of steel, tightening, ever tightening their deadly grip.
Then, as suddenly as it had started, it was over. The rat man quivered and lay still.

read_agent_gists:
COMING OF THE GODS
By CHESTER WHITEHORN

Ro, familiar with Mars' dangerous jungles, cautiously returned home after exploring strange northern villages. Weary of adventure, he longed to see his family and Na, his love. Reaching the plain, he smiled, but halted when spotting a figure being chased.

Ro studied the approaching figures, gasping when he recognized the pursued woman. The pursuer was a squat, ugly Oan rat man. Ro's face turned grim as he picked up a rock and ran towards them. The Oan, focused on his prey, didn't notice Ro until it was too late. Ro threw the rock, hitting the Oan in the side and killing him. The woman, glad to be saved, embraced Ro, who couldn't help but smile despite trying to appear stern.

"You're home," breathed Na.
"I've returned from the north, having seen many things," answered Ro. "Tell me first about playing tag with the Oan."
Na looked down. "I was caught in the forest and ran from the Oan. The chase was long and tiring. You appeared just in time."
"You were alone?" Ro exclaimed. "Women don't travel alone from the cliffs."
"Since a long time," she answered sadly, then cried. "Many weeks ago, a metal sphere landed in the valley with a loud noise. Our men went to greet it, carrying stones. Two white men stepped out, holding weapons. They communicated telepathically, saying they came from Earth in peace. Two more arrived: a golden-haired woman and a silver-haired man. They showed us their weapons, and we celebrated."

That night, they asked their friends to join them in the caves, but the friends camped in the valley. With dawn, they found their friends captured and killed by the Oan using weapons. A three-day battle ensued, and they fled under cover of night. Ro, upon hearing this, vowed revenge, determined to free the captured white men and use their weapons against the Oan. Na, concerned, warned of the Oan's numbers. Ro, undeterred, planned to rescue the white men and learn more about them. As night fell, he bid farewell to Na, marking her as his mate before leaving to face the Oan.

Ro carried stones in his left hand and under his armpit, ready to attack any Oan who crossed his path. His right arm was free for climbing, finding crevices and footholds in the dark. The long, dangerous climb left him sweating, but he reached the bottom and moved cautiously toward the camp, avoiding detection. Ahead, he saw a clearing where he would find the white ones. Suddenly, a guard approached. Remembering his mother's warning about the Oan's keen eyesight, Ro struck the guard silently, ensuring no alarm was raised.

Breathing easier, Ro silently reached the clearing's edge. On the ground, slumbering rat men lay. He lay flat, inching forward cautiously. Scratched but painless, he felt victory near. Spotting a golden mass, he freed a girl who communicated telepathically. Urging her to stay still, he promised to signal when ready. Freeing her, he moved to untie others, growing more confident.

When ready, Ro led the four white people into the woods. The girl whispered, and Ro glared. They moved slowly at first but sped up as silence became less crucial. Ro explained their route and revealed Na as his chosen mate. The man with silver hair questioned Ro's rescue motives, to which Ro replied he aimed to avenge his family and tribe. They waited for Na but encountered a rat man carrying a girl instead. Ro attacked, overpowering the rat man in a fierce struggle, ultimately killing him.

Ro dismounted the limp body, his face shifting from triumph to concern as he remembered the girl. He rushed to her side as she opened her eyes, fearfully looking around before smiling at him. Na recounted her mistake, thinking the rat man was Ro. After comforting her, they traveled to a hidden cave, where Ro assured them they'd stay until ready to attack the Oan. Despite the six against hundreds, Ro remained confident, planning to discuss further at dawn. He observed the group, noting the wise old man, the handsome but pale men, and the beautiful girl with golden hair.

"Tell me, where is this strange place you come from, and how can you speak with minds?" the old man asked.

"We're from Earth, millions of miles away. We're an expedition to study Mars," the lean man, Carlson, and the stouter man, Grimm, introduced themselves along with their leader.

"What's Earth like?" Ro asked.

"Our part, America, has steel and stone cities, and we travel in space boats. What year is it here?" the old man inquired.

"You've seen it," Ro replied. "We don't measure time by years."

"A year is a time measure. It was 2200 when we left Earth," the old man explained.

"We don't have that here," Ro said. "But how do you speak with minds?"

"It's telepathy. We learn it from childhood. It's like sending pictures in a universal language," the old man said.

Ro sighed

Ro slipped his arm around Na's shoulder as they slept. Upon waking, he found her gone and scolded her for wandering, though he helped her with the fruits and berries she had gathered. Noticing Charlotte's shyness and Carlson's annoyance, Ro realized the men were no different from Martians in their treatment of women. After breakfast, Carlson questioned their plan to attack the Oan. Ro, angered by Grimm's claim that they lacked the resources to make weapons, was calmed by the professor's suggestion to use weapons from their ship, though they faced the challenge of reaching it due to its heavy guard.

Ro fell silent, then proposed a plan to the group. He suggested Na could lure the Oan guards away by revealing herself, allowing others to take the unguarded sphere. The professor objected, but Ro assured he would protect Na from above while Grimm and Carlson would assist. After a brief altercation, they agreed on roles and left the cave to head towards the Oan camp.

They moved swiftly to the narrow valley entrance, about a hundred yards long and twenty feet wide, with steep cliffs on both sides. Ro left the professor with Na, who would guide him to the sphere. Ro was proud of Na's fearlessness. Carlson watched Charlotte with a similar expression. Ro urged them to keep moving to avoid Oan strategies. They climbed in silence, and Ro paused to look back, feeling regret as Na and the others disappeared. At the top, Ro and Carlson prepared a rockfall trap over the valley's narrowest part. Ro teased Carlson about his feelings for Charlotte, suggesting fear of Grimm was the reason for not saying goodbye.

Carlson, after considering Ro's words, admitted he and Charlotte planned to marry in America, not seeing Grimm as a threat. Ro suggested marrying on Mars to end the conflict, pointing out that legality and ceremonies were irrelevant on Mars. Ro believed a kiss and a promise were sufficient. Carlson agreed, noting that while it might seem chaotic by Earth standards, it was likely binding on Mars. Ro remained silent, finding the situation more pressing than unraveling the complexities of Earth's marriage customs.

query:
What was most likely the strongest motivator for humans to develop telepathy?

 (A) Telepathy takes less concentration than speaking aloud. 
 (B) Telepathy is ideal for keeping sensitive information secret, since it cannot be accidentally overheard. 
 (C) Telepathy enables communication across language barriers. 
 (D) Telepathy eliminates the misunderstanding that comes with words.
references: Telepathy enables communication across language barriers.

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
"Tell me," he asked suddenly, "where is this strange place you come from? And how is it that you can speak and cause others to speak with their minds?"
It was the old man who answered.
"We come from a place called Earth, many millions of miles away through space. My daughter, Charlotte, my two assistants, Carlson—" the lean man nodded—"Grimm—" the stouter man acknowledged the introduction—"and myself are an expedition. We came here to Mars to study."
Ro introduced himself and Na.
"What manner of a place is this Earth?" he asked, after the formalities.
"Our part of Earth, America, is a great country. Our cities are built of steel and stone, and we travel about in space boats. Now tell me, what is it like here on Mars? Surely the whole planet isn't wilderness. What year is it?"
"You have seen what it is like here," Ro answered. "As for 'year,' I don't understand."
"A year is a measure of time," the old man explained. "When we left Earth it was the year twenty-two hundred."
"We have nothing like that here," said Ro, still puzzled. "But tell me, about this speaking with the mind. Perhaps I shall understand that."
"It's simple telepathy. We have mastered the science on Earth. It takes study from childhood, but once you have mastered the art, it is quite simple to transmit or receive thoughts from anyone. A mere matter of concentration. We—who speak different tongues—understand each other because of action we have in mind as we speak. We want the other to walk, we think of the other walking. A picture is transmitted and understood. It is a message in a Universal language."
Ro sighed.
"I am afraid we are very backward here on Mars," he said wearily. "I would like to learn more, but we must sleep now. Tomorrow will be a very busy day."

"A year is a measure of time," the old man explained. "When we left Earth it was the year twenty-two hundred."
"We have nothing like that here," said Ro, still puzzled. "But tell me, about this speaking with the mind. Perhaps I shall understand that."
"It's simple telepathy. We have mastered the science on Earth. It takes study from childhood, but once you have mastered the art, it is quite simple to transmit or receive thoughts from anyone. A mere matter of concentration. We—who speak different tongues—understand each other because of action we have in mind as we speak. We want the other to walk, we think of the other walking. A picture is transmitted and understood. It is a message in a Universal language."
Ro sighed.
"I am afraid we are very backward here on Mars," he said wearily. "I would like to learn more, but we must sleep now. Tomorrow will be a very busy day."
Ro slipped his arm around Na's shoulder as they slept. Upon waking, he found her gone and scolded her for wandering, though he helped her with the fruits and berries she had gathered. Noticing Charlotte's shyness and Carlson's annoyance, Ro realized the men were no different from Martians in their treatment of women. After breakfast, Carlson questioned their plan to attack the Oan. Ro, angered by Grimm's claim that they lacked the resources to make weapons, was calmed by the professor's suggestion to use weapons from their ship, though they faced the challenge of reaching it due to its heavy guard.
Ro fell silent, then proposed a plan to the group. He suggested Na could lure the Oan guards away by revealing herself, allowing others to take the unguarded sphere. The professor objected, but Ro assured he would protect Na from above while Grimm and Carlson would assist. After a brief altercation, they agreed on roles and left the cave to head towards the Oan camp.
They moved swiftly to the narrow valley entrance, about a hundred yards long and twenty feet wide, with steep cliffs on both sides. Ro left the professor with Na, who would guide him to the sphere. Ro was proud of Na's fearlessness. Carlson watched Charlotte with a similar expression. Ro urged them to keep moving to avoid Oan strategies. They climbed in silence, and Ro paused to look back, feeling regret as Na and the others disappeared. At the top, Ro and Carlson prepared a rockfall trap over the valley's narrowest part. Ro teased Carlson about his feelings for Charlotte, suggesting fear of Grimm was the reason for not saying goodbye.
Carlson, after considering Ro's words, admitted he and Charlotte planned to marry in America, not seeing Grimm as a threat. Ro suggested marrying on Mars to end the conflict, pointing out that legality and ceremonies were irrelevant on Mars. Ro believed a kiss and a promise were sufficient. Carlson agreed, noting that while it might seem chaotic by Earth standards, it was likely binding on Mars. Ro remained silent, finding the situation more pressing than unraveling the complexities of Earth's marriage customs.

What was most likely the strongest motivator for humans to develop telepathy?

 (A) Telepathy takes less concentration than speaking aloud. 
 (B) Telepathy is ideal for keeping sensitive information secret, since it cannot be accidentally overheard. 
 (C) Telepathy enables communication across language barriers. 
 (D) Telepathy eliminates the misunderstanding that comes with words.

Answer with ONLY the exact text of the correct option, without the option letter, parentheses, or any extra words.
prediction: Telepathy enables communication across language barriers.

What became of Ro's mother?

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
COMING OF THE GODS
By CHESTER WHITEHORN
Never had Mars seen such men as these, for they
came from black space, carrying weird weapons—to
fight for a race of which they had never heard.
Ro moved cautiously. He knew the jungles of Mars well, knew the dangers, the swift death that could come to an unwary traveler. Many times he had seen fellow Martians die by the razor fangs of Gin, the swamp snake. Their clear red skin had become blotched and purple, their eyeballs popped, their faces swollen by the poison that raced through their veins. And Ro had seen the bones of luckless men vomited from the mouths of the Droo, the cannibal plants. And others there had been, some friends of his, who had become game for beasts of prey, or been swallowed by hungry, sucking pools of quicksand. No, the jungles of Mars were not to be taken casually, no matter how light in heart one was at the prospect of seeing home once more.
Ro was returning from the north. He had seen the great villages of thatched huts, the strange people who lived in these huts instead of in caves, and wore coverings on their feet and shining rings in their ears. And having quenched his curiosity about these people and their villages, he was satisfied to travel home again.
He was a man of the world now, weary of exploring and ready to settle down. He was anxious to see his family again, his father and mother and all his brothers and sisters; to sit round a fire with them at the entrance to their cave and tell of the wondrous places he'd visited. And, most of all, he wanted to see Na, graceful, dark eyed Na, whose fair face had disturbed his slumber so often, appearing in his dreams to call him home.
He breathed a sigh of relief as he reached the jungle's edge. Before him lay a broad expanse of plain. And far in the distance rose the great cliffs and the hills that were his home.
His handsome face broadened into a smile and he quickened his pace to a trot. There was no need for caution now. The dangers on the plain were few.
The sun beat down on his bare head and back. His red skin glistened. His thick black hair shone healthily.
Mile after mile fell behind him. His long, well muscled legs carried him swiftly toward the distant hills. His movements were graceful, easy, as the loping of Shee, the great cat.
Then, suddenly, he faltered in his stride. He stopped running and, shielding his eyes from the sun's glare, stared ahead. There was a figure running toward him. And behind that first figure, a second gave chase.

In his left hand and under his armpit Ro carried stones. They were of a good weight and would make short work of any Oan who was foolish enough to cross his path.
His right arm he kept free for climbing. His fingers found crevices to hold to in the almost smooth wall. His toes seemed to have eyes to pierce the darkness in finding footholds.
The climb was long and dangerous. Ro's skin glistened with sweat. He had lived in the cliffs all his life, and had made many perilous climbs, but never one on so dark a night. It seemed an eternity before he rested at the bottom.
Feeling his way cautiously, he moved toward the camp. He could sense the presence of many Oan close by. The hair at the base of his neck prickled. He prayed he wouldn't be seen. An alarm now would spoil his plan.
Ahead of him, he saw a clearing. That would be his destination. On the far side he would find the white ones. He took the stone from his armpit and moved on.
Suddenly he halted. A dim figure approached. It was one of the Oan, a guard. He was coming straight at Ro. The young Martian shrank back.
"The rat men have eyes to cut the night." It was a memory of his mother's voice. She had spoken those words when he was a child, to keep him from straying too far.
The Oan was only a few feet away now, but his eyes were not cutting the night. Ro could see his large ears, hear his twitching tail. In a moment the beast would stumble over him.
Like a phantom, Ro arose from his crouch. The rat man was startled, frozen with fear. Ro drove his right arm around. The stone in his hand cracked the Oan's skull like an eggshell. Ro caught the body as it fell, lowered it noiselessly to the ground.

read_agent_gists:
COMING OF THE GODS
By CHESTER WHITEHORN

Ro, familiar with Mars' dangerous jungles, cautiously returned home after exploring strange northern villages. Weary of adventure, he longed to see his family and Na, his love. Reaching the plain, he smiled, but halted when spotting a figure being chased.

Ro studied the approaching figures, gasping when he recognized the pursued woman. The pursuer was a squat, ugly Oan rat man. Ro's face turned grim as he picked up a rock and ran towards them. The Oan, focused on his prey, didn't notice Ro until it was too late. Ro threw the rock, hitting the Oan in the side and killing him. The woman, glad to be saved, embraced Ro, who couldn't help but smile despite trying to appear stern.

"You're home," breathed Na.
"I've returned from the north, having seen many things," answered Ro. "Tell me first about playing tag with the Oan."
Na looked down. "I was caught in the forest and ran from the Oan. The chase was long and tiring. You appeared just in time."
"You were alone?" Ro exclaimed. "Women don't travel alone from the cliffs."
"Since a long time," she answered sadly, then cried. "Many weeks ago, a metal sphere landed in the valley with a loud noise. Our men went to greet it, carrying stones. Two white men stepped out, holding weapons. They communicated telepathically, saying they came from Earth in peace. Two more arrived: a golden-haired woman and a silver-haired man. They showed us their weapons, and we celebrated."

That night, they asked their friends to join them in the caves, but the friends camped in the valley. With dawn, they found their friends captured and killed by the Oan using weapons. A three-day battle ensued, and they fled under cover of night. Ro, upon hearing this, vowed revenge, determined to free the captured white men and use their weapons against the Oan. Na, concerned, warned of the Oan's numbers. Ro, undeterred, planned to rescue the white men and learn more about them. As night fell, he bid farewell to Na, marking her as his mate before leaving to face the Oan.

Ro carried stones in his left hand and under his armpit, ready to attack any Oan who crossed his path. His right arm was free for climbing, finding crevices and footholds in the dark. The long, dangerous climb left him sweating, but he reached the bottom and moved cautiously toward the camp, avoiding detection. Ahead, he saw a clearing where he would find the white ones. Suddenly, a guard approached. Remembering his mother's warning about the Oan's keen eyesight, Ro struck the guard silently, ensuring no alarm was raised.

Breathing easier, Ro silently reached the clearing's edge. On the ground, slumbering rat men lay. He lay flat, inching forward cautiously. Scratched but painless, he felt victory near. Spotting a golden mass, he freed a girl who communicated telepathically. Urging her to stay still, he promised to signal when ready. Freeing her, he moved to untie others, growing more confident.

When ready, Ro led the four white people into the woods. The girl whispered, and Ro glared. They moved slowly at first but sped up as silence became less crucial. Ro explained their route and revealed Na as his chosen mate. The man with silver hair questioned Ro's rescue motives, to which Ro replied he aimed to avenge his family and tribe. They waited for Na but encountered a rat man carrying a girl instead. Ro attacked, overpowering the rat man in a fierce struggle, ultimately killing him.

Ro dismounted the limp body, his face shifting from triumph to concern as he remembered the girl. He rushed to her side as she opened her eyes, fearfully looking around before smiling at him. Na recounted her mistake, thinking the rat man was Ro. After comforting her, they traveled to a hidden cave, where Ro assured them they'd stay until ready to attack the Oan. Despite the six against hundreds, Ro remained confident, planning to discuss further at dawn. He observed the group, noting the wise old man, the handsome but pale men, and the beautiful girl with golden hair.

"Tell me, where is this strange place you come from, and how can you speak with minds?" the old man asked.

"We're from Earth, millions of miles away. We're an expedition to study Mars," the lean man, Carlson, and the stouter man, Grimm, introduced themselves along with their leader.

"What's Earth like?" Ro asked.

"Our part, America, has steel and stone cities, and we travel in space boats. What year is it here?" the old man inquired.

"You've seen it," Ro replied. "We don't measure time by years."

"A year is a time measure. It was 2200 when we left Earth," the old man explained.

"We don't have that here," Ro said. "But how do you speak with minds?"

"It's telepathy. We learn it from childhood. It's like sending pictures in a universal language," the old man said.

Ro sighed

Ro slipped his arm around Na's shoulder as they slept. Upon waking, he found her gone and scolded her for wandering, though he helped her with the fruits and berries she had gathered. Noticing Charlotte's shyness and Carlson's annoyance, Ro realized the men were no different from Martians in their treatment of women. After breakfast, Carlson questioned their plan to attack the Oan. Ro, angered by Grimm's claim that they lacked the resources to make weapons, was calmed by the professor's suggestion to use weapons from their ship, though they faced the challenge of reaching it due to its heavy guard.

Ro fell silent, then proposed a plan to the group. He suggested Na could lure the Oan guards away by revealing herself, allowing others to take the unguarded sphere. The professor objected, but Ro assured he would protect Na from above while Grimm and Carlson would assist. After a brief altercation, they agreed on roles and left the cave to head towards the Oan camp.

They moved swiftly to the narrow valley entrance, about a hundred yards long and twenty feet wide, with steep cliffs on both sides. Ro left the professor with Na, who would guide him to the sphere. Ro was proud of Na's fearlessness. Carlson watched Charlotte with a similar expression. Ro urged them to keep moving to avoid Oan strategies. They climbed in silence, and Ro paused to look back, feeling regret as Na and the others disappeared. At the top, Ro and Carlson prepared a rockfall trap over the valley's narrowest part. Ro teased Carlson about his feelings for Charlotte, suggesting fear of Grimm was the reason for not saying goodbye.

Carlson, after considering Ro's words, admitted he and Charlotte planned to marry in America, not seeing Grimm as a threat. Ro suggested marrying on Mars to end the conflict, pointing out that legality and ceremonies were irrelevant on Mars. Ro believed a kiss and a promise were sufficient. Carlson agreed, noting that while it might seem chaotic by Earth standards, it was likely binding on Mars. Ro remained silent, finding the situation more pressing than unraveling the complexities of Earth's marriage customs.
